title: Post-call recap — propose two 30-min blocks for product intro
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Sophie Turner <sophie.turner@cardinalanalytics.com>, Alex Mendez <alex@cardinalanalytics.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 4 Dec 2026 15:12:00 -0800
Subject: Post-call recap — propose two 30-min blocks for product intro

Hi Sophie / Alex —

Thanks again for the call earlier today. Quick recap: Cardinal is evaluating inference options to improve throughput for short-chat agents and reduce per-token cost. You mentioned 95% of requests are < 256 tokens and latency targets are ~100–150ms p50.

Next step: a 30‑minute intro demo to show the product and a short infra/latency discussion. To keep this tight I suggest:

- Option A: Tue Dec 7 — 10:00–10:30 PT (17:00–17:30 UTC)
- Option B: Wed Dec 8 — 14:00–14:30 PT (21:00–21:30 UTC)
- Alternate: Thu Dec 9 — 09:00–09:30 PT (16:00–16:30 UTC)

Could you confirm which slot works best and who from your side we should expect (product manager, engineering, or both)? Jonas (our Solutions Engineer) will join for the infra/latency Q&A.

Attached: RDW_one_pager.pdf and a short demo agenda. Also here’s a quick Calendly link if you prefer to pick a slot: https://calendly.com/redwood/30min-intro

If none of these work, tell me two 30‑minute windows that do and I’ll follow up with a calendar invite.

Thanks —
Ava Chen
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | (415) 555-0172

Attachments: RDW_one_pager.pdf


From: Sophie Turner <sophie.turner@cardinalanalytics.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Alex Mendez <alex@cardinalanalytics.com>
Date: Fri, 4 Dec 2026 17:03:00 -0500
Subject: Re: Post-call recap — propose two 30-min blocks for product intro

Thanks, Ava —

Appreciate the recap. Option A (Tue Dec 7 at 10am PT) looks promising for our group. I will be on the call and I’ll invite Alex (engineering lead). Two quick asks before we lock:

1) Can your SE touch briefly on data residency and whether we can pin inference to a US-only region?
2) Can you confirm expected p50 latency for short-chat traffic with caching enabled?

If Jonas can handle those items, we’re good to go. Please send a calendar invite and include the dial-in/Zoom link.

Best,
Sophie Turner
Head of Product | Cardinal Analytics
Sophie.Turner@cardinalanalytics.com

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Sophie Turner <sophie.turner@cardinalanalytics.com>, Alex Mendez <alex@cardinalanalytics.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 5 Dec 2026 08:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-call recap — propose two 30-min blocks for product intro

Great — locking in Tue Dec 7 at 10:00 PT. I’ll send a calendar invite shortly.

Agenda (20/10 split):
- 0–5 min: Quick intros + brief product positioning (Ava)
- 5–25 min: Live demo focused on short-chat flows + cost/latency profile (Jonas)
- 25–30 min: Q&A / next steps (all)

Jonas: can you be prepared to show typical p50/p95 numbers with our small-prefix cache enabled and comment on region pinning and KMS options?

Also attaching a short demo-agenda.md that we’ll use as a reference.

Thanks, Sophie — I’ll send the invite with a Teams link and include a Drive folder for post-demo artifacts.

— Ava

Attachments: demo-agenda.md


From: Alex Mendez <alex@cardinalanalytics.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Sophie Turner <sophie.turner@cardinalanalytics.com>
Date: Sat, 5 Dec 2026 09:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-call recap — propose two 30-min blocks for product intro

Thanks — Dec 7 at 10 PT works for me. I’ll loop in two engineers (infra + backend) to join the 10am slot.

A quick ask on our side: could you add a 1–2 item pre-demo checklist in the invite so we can confirm data access (sample logs or a small staging dataset) and expected auth flow? Means we can get the right people on the call and speed up the follow-up POC conversation.

Sophie — I’ll invite Maria (eng) + Daniel (infra) to the calendar once Ava sends the invite.

- Alex

title: Supplier payment profile & buyer intake kickoff — Quick checklist
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Patel <samir@greenloop.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 02 Dec 2026 15:12:00 -0800
Subject: Supplier payment profile & buyer intake kickoff — Quick checklist

Hi Samir —

Thanks for confirming GreenLoop as an approved vendor. To get payments and POs flowing we need a short vendor profile + banking remittance details. Below is a checklist so we can provision a supplier code and route POs correctly. If you can return the items in one reply it speeds up provisioning.

Required items (quick):
- Legal entity name (exact as on tax form) and entity type (Corp, LLC, Partnership)
- Remit-to address for PO & invoices (line1, city, state, zip, country)
- Tax ID (EIN or VAT) and signed W-9/W-8 (depending on entity) — attach as PDF
- Preferred payment method: ACH (US), Wire (international), or Check
- Bank details for ACH/wire: bank name, routing (ABA) + account number (or IBAN/SWIFT for wire) — we can accept a bank letter instead of full account number for initial setup if preferred
- PO routing / cost center info (who should POs be routed to inside GreenLoop)
- Any special invoice terms (net30, net45, invoice header requirements)

Attached: Supplier_Profile_Template.xlsx — fill the simple sheet and return with the supporting docs. Once we have the files, Finance will kick off micro-deposit verification (ACH) or request wire test for cross-border accounts.

Timeline: If we receive everything by end of day Friday we can provision a supplier code and enable PO acceptance within 2 business days.

Thanks —

Ava Chen
Senior Sales Manager, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0192
Redwood Inference

Attachment: Supplier_Profile_Template.xlsx (spreadsheet)


From: Samir Patel <samir@greenloop.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 02 Dec 2026 16:04:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Supplier payment profile & buyer intake kickoff — Quick checklist

Ava / Ben —

Thanks — attaching the essentials now. Quick answers inline:

- Legal entity: GreenLoop Analytics, Inc. (C-Corp)
- Remit-to address: 1200 Harbor Drive, Suite 400, San Mateo, CA 94401, USA
- Tax ID: EIN 12-3456789 (attached signed W-9)
- Preferred payment: ACH (US) — we prefer ACH for recurring payments
- Bank details: Will attach bank letter and partial account info. We prefer not to email full account numbers; attached is GreenLoop_bank_letter.pdf and a masked-account snapshot (last 4: ****6789). If finance needs full ACH details we can provide via a secure portal or phone.
- PO routing: Please route POs to Billing / AP — contact: finance@greenloop.com (cc'd)
- Invoice terms: Net30 standard; invoice header must include PO number and project code (GL-PRJ-001).

Attached: GreenLoop_W9_signed.pdf, GreenLoop_bank_letter.pdf, Supplier_Profile_Filled.xlsx

Samir Patel
Head of Ops, GreenLoop Analytics
samir@greenloop.com | +1 (650) 555-2233

On Wed, Dec 2, 2026 at 3:12 PM Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai> wrote:
> [original checklist]


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 02 Dec 2026 17:02:00 -08:00
Subject: Fwd: Supplier payment profile & attachments — GreenLoop Analytics

Looping in procurement/ops: Samir uploaded the W-9 and a bank letter but prefers not to email full account numbers. Priyom / Neha — can Finance accept the bank letter + DocuSign portal exchange for ACH details? If so, please provision a supplier code and set PO routing as follows:

- Cost center / GL routing: Cloud Ops (cost center 7221)
- Default invoice terms: Net30, require PO on invoice
- Payment method: ACH (primary); wire fallback for one-off cross-border payments

Action items:
1) Priyom: confirm micro-deposit workflow and whether we need full account numbers or if we can use the bank letter + secure portal. Estimate time-to-pay once micro-deposit verified.
2) Neha: create supplier code in AP system (include GreenLoop Banking note + attach W-9).
3) Ava: confirm to Samir that we have the PO routing and next steps for micro-deposit.

Thanks —
Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai

Attachment: (copied attachments from Samir) GreenLoop_W9_signed.pdf, GreenLoop_bank_letter.pdf


From: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Patel <samir@greenloop.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 03 Dec 2026 09:15:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Supplier payment profile & buyer intake kickoff — Micro-deposit step

Hi Samir —

Thanks for the prompt turnaround. We can accept the bank letter plus a secure portal upload for full account numbers. Options:

A) GreenLoop uploads full ACH details via our secure vendor portal (we will send a one-time secure link) — Finance will then initiate two micro-deposits (amounts < $0.50) for verification. Once Samir confirms micro-deposit amounts we mark ACH as verified and enable payments.

B) If you prefer not to use a portal, we can arrange a short phone call to capture the account details (we'll redact in our records afterwards).

Next steps from our side:
- Neha will create the supplier code (AP ID) and attach the W-9 and bank letter.
- I will generate and send the secure portal link (expires in 48 hours).
- Please confirm the exact remit-to address for PO printing (we have the one you provided; confirm if PO should reference Attn: Billing).

Estimated timeline: portal upload + micro-deposit flow typically completes in 1–3 business days. If you reply here confirming portal upload preference we'll send the link immediately.

Best,
Priyom Das
Procurement Ops, Redwood Inference
priyom.das@redwood.ai

Attachment: bank_letter_sample.pdf (example format)


From: Samir Patel <samir@greenloop.com>
To: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 03 Dec 2026 10:41:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Supplier payment profile & buyer intake kickoff — Micro-deposit step

Priyom / Ava / Ben —

Portal sounds good — please send the secure link and I will have our finance director upload the full ACH details today. Confirming remit-to should be:

GreenLoop Analytics, Inc.
Attn: Billing — AP Team
1200 Harbor Drive, Suite 400
San Mateo, CA 94401
USA

Once you send the portal link we'll upload and expect micro-deposits within 1–2 business days. Thank you for the quick turnaround.

Samir
Samir Patel
Head of Ops, GreenLoop Analytics
samir@greenloop.com
+1 (650) 555-2233

title: NovusAI dedicated migration: sizing validation sprint & contingency cadence
preview:
From: Nora Patel <nora@novusai.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-29T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Sizing ask for hosted→dedicated migration

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the product walkthrough last week. We just landed board approval to proceed with a Dedicated deployment for NovusAI's inference workloads and need to lock down sizing and a short validation/cutover window.

High level:
- Current hosted baseline: ~3.2M tokens/day, peak sustained qps ~1.8k for our chat endpoint (mostly 1–2k token responses).
- We expect seasonal spikes up to ~3.5k qps for two hour windows (Black Friday-ish).
- Required uptime SLA during business hours (PST): 99.95% to start.

Based on the hosted export, preliminary sizing suggestions:
- Baseline fleet: 6x A10G (or equivalent T4x2) worker nodes (8 GPUs total via multi-GPU pods) with model shard + KV cache enabled to hit the 1.8k qps baseline with p95 latency ~120–180ms for 2k-token responses.
- Buffer for spikes: design to absorb +2.0x concurrency for short bursts (autoscale up to 12x A10G equivalent).
- Cost/path trade: recommend keeping a quantized 4-bit variant for non-critical routes — saves ~40% GPU cycles during peak.

Validation plan (48h replay):
1) Stage mirror: replay 72h of production traffic at 1.0x velocity for sanity.
2) Soak test: 24h at 1.5x baseline (sustained) to confirm thermal and throughput characteristics.
3) Spike drills: automated bursts to 2.0x for 15–30min to validate autoscaling and fallback to hosted API if node-level issues occur.

Acceptance gates (short list): p95 latency <250ms, error rate <0.1%, KV hit rate >85%, no OOM events for 48h.

Attachments: capacity_matrix_v1.xlsx (I included per-route recommended GPU mapping) and inference-benchmarks.pdf with kernel-level numbers.

Quick answers and details for the runbook:
Rollback timing and impact:
- Hot rollback to hosted: target 20–45 minutes end-to-end for DNS cutover + client-side retry window, assuming the hosted endpoints are warm. We'll keep an active warmpool on hosted for the cutover weekend.
- Data plane: no database schema changes or data migrations involved; routing switch only. Ensure the client library can tolerate 409s/retries for up to 60s.

Validation instrumentation (we'll ship a metrics bundle you can drop into your Prometheus):
- Key metrics: p50/p95/p99 latency by route, error rate by model variant, KV hit rate, GPU utilization, OOMs, and request fanout size.
- Logging: sample-capture for 1% of traffic to verify outputs for quality regression checks.

Cutover cadence (high level):
- T-7d: final smoke tests in staging, runbook dry-run with on-call rotation.
- T-2d: deploy dedicated infra and dry-route a small % (5%) of traffic for 4h.
- T-0d (cutover): 02:00–03:00 PST maintenance window, controlled migration in 4 waves (10% → 30% → 60% → 100%) with 30m observation between waves.
- Rollback: immediate on acceptance criteria breach or sustained error spike; execute hot rollback and call the incident bridge.

I'll drop the draft runbook link: https://drive.google.com/drive/fake-link/novusai-dedicated-runbook (see 'dedicated-cutover-runbook.pdf' attachment).

Karthik

title: Re: HelixHealth pilot — subprocessor certification path & TIA rollback controls
preview:
From: Samuel Gray <samuel.gray@helixhealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: HelixHealth Legal <legal@helixhealth.com>
Date: Thu, 26 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0500
Subject: Subprocessor attestation + TIA rollback controls for UK pilot

Hi Ava —

Thanks again for the demo yesterday. Our legal & privacy team has a few follow-ups before we can move the pilot into production in the UK:

1) Subprocessor certification: We need a list of any third-party subprocessors involved in request routing, on-prem connectors, and logging/exfiltration tools, plus a short attestation from each that they can comply with the UK Addendum (IDTA) security obligations.

2) TIA rollback controls: If we rely on Transitional Impact Assessments (TIAs) for an interim routing path, HelixHealth needs explicit rollback controls — i.e., how you'd revert to onshore-only processing if an issue is detected, and how quickly (SLA) that full rollback happens. Also, evidence of testing this rollback (runbook or test results) would be ideal.

3) Evidence handoff cadence: we prefer a staged evidence handoff (initial attestation within 5 business days, full artifacts within 3 weeks). Can you confirm whether Redwood can meet that cadence and whether any items will be redacted for security reasons?

I attached the HelixHealth questionnaire we use to capture subprocessor info. Can you route to your security/compliance team and let us know next steps?

Best,
Sam

Attachment: helix_subprocessor_questionnaire.xlsx (spreadsheet)

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Samuel Gray <samuel.gray@helixhealth.com>
Cc: HelixHealth Legal <legal@helixhealth.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 26 Nov 2026 11:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Subprocessor attestation + TIA rollback controls for UK pilot

Hi Sam —

Thanks for the clear list. We're aligned on the ask and can meet the cadence you proposed with one caveat (see \"redactions\" below). Here's what I propose as an action plan: 

- Day 1 (today): Acknowledgement and initial point-of-contact (this email).
- Day 3: Subprocessor roster + short-form attestations (we'll provide names, roles, and attestation statements).
- Day 14: Full attestation artifacts, technical excerpts (network flow diagrams, key rotation schedules) and a documented rollback runbook.
- Day 21: Evidence of rollback test (redacted test logs + vendor test summary).

Redactions: Some vendor test logs include infrastructure IPs and internal ticket IDs. We can provide redacted logs that still show the test results and timestamps. Anything we can't share will be summarized by our security engineer and flagged for HelixHealth Legal to review.

TIA rollback controls (summary):
- Rollback trigger criteria: degraded routing to onshore (<95% success for 10 mins), cryptographic verification failures, or vendor attestation revocation.
- Rollback path: Automatic routing policy change within our smart router + immediate KV cache flush for affected sessions.
- Target rollback time: automated fallback within 6 minutes; full traffic verification and confirmation within 2 hours (manual sign-off).
- Test evidence: We'll run a staged rollback test in our staging environment and provide a test packet trace and short test report.

I've looped in Naomi (Security) and Kimberly (Compliance) to coordinate the attestation drafts. We'll aim to upload the initial packages to the Drive link below.

Drive: https://drive.google.com/open?id=1a2b3cHelixEvidence

Cheers,
Ava Chen
Director, Solutions — Redwood Inference

--
Ava Chen
Director, Solutions
Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwoodinference.com | +1 (415) 555-0192

Marcus (ops) confirmed two downstreams that are still under NDA; we can list them as \"redacted_subprocessor_A (NDA)\" with a summary of controls and offer to provide unredacted attestation under Helix's NDA. Kimberly, please confirm whether we require an executed MoU before submitting the full artifacts.

2) Attestation language: We should use a standard short-form template that maps directly to Section 5 of the Redwood DPA + UK Addendum clauses 3.1–3.5. Marissa, can you draft the short-form attestation and include a checkbox matrix for key commitments (encryption at rest/in transit, access logs, breach notification timeline)?

3) Rollback test artifacts: We will not share raw packet captures. Instead, we'll provide a redacted packet timeline and a cryptographic checksum of a sample trace (so Helix can verify the sample without seeing payloads). Also include the runbook steps and test time windows.

4) Timeline risk: 14-day full artifact delivery assumes no legal gating from our downstream vendors. If a vendor requires additional review, we should surface this to Helix immediately and propose a temporary mitigant (e.g., stricter routing to dedicated onshore pool).

I'll prepare the redacted artifact checklist and send to Ava/Kimberly by end of day.

- Naomi

Hi Samir — thanks again for the conversation earlier. Quick recap + proposed next steps so we stay aligned: 

1) Summary of ask: MediTech is evaluating Redwood for clinical note generation at scale. Key gating items you flagged: preferred purchase flow (PO vs card vs marketplace), SOC 2 evidence and DPA, and an outstanding security questionnaire from your procurement team.

2) Procurement options we can support:
   - Standard PO/invoice + monthly billing (preferred for enterprise teams)
   - Corporate card (fastest to start, lower paperwork)
   - Cloud marketplace procurement (Azure Marketplace or GCP Marketplace) — useful if you want consolidated cloud billing and marketplace contract terms

3) Security/compliance: we can provide SOC 2 Type II report, a DPA template, and a completed security questionnaire. We typically deliver SOC 2 artifacts under an NDA or post-LOE; DPA is straightforward to execute when the SOW/contract is in place.

4) Proposal for next steps:
   - Samir: confirm preferred procurement route (PO vs card vs marketplace) and whether Procurement needs a vendor form or SSO/SCIM setup details.
   - Lydia/CISO: if possible, share your priority security questions (we can prioritize responses in 48–72 hours).
   - Marissa: we will prepare a draft SOW and PO-friendly invoice schedule once route is confirmed.

Attachments: Redwood_SOC2_TypeII_2026.pdf (SOC 2 summary), Redwood_DPA_Summary_2026.pdf (DPA highlights).

Happy to hop on a short call if that helps.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0192

- Timeline: PO start to production onboarding usually takes 2–4 weeks (depending on signature and SSO setup). Card-based pilots can start in 2–3 business days. Marketplace adds ~4–8 weeks.

- Billing cadence: we support monthly invoices net-30 by default for PO customers. Annual prepay is also supported for committed capacity.

Next best step: if PO is the chosen route, send the PO template and we’ll provide a formal quote and draft SOW for signature. I’ll also drop a DocuSign link for the DPA if legal prefers that channel.

Hi Ava,

Can you include:
- Short description of the sampling method used
- The time window examined
- Whether any customer identifiers or PII were present in the sample
- Any attachments or output we can use for our audit (hash manifest or sample csv)

Best,
Elena Morales
Head of Security Operations
FinBlue

---
Elena Morales
FinBlue | elena@finblue.com
Phone: +1 415 555 0134

From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Elena Morales <elena@finblue.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 24 Nov 2026 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Request: quick confirmation on log sampling results

Hi Elena — thanks for the call.

Short answer up front: our sampling and hash-reconciliation results show no evidence of FinBlue customer records being accessed or exfiltrated during the incident window. See details below and attachments.

What we examined
- Time window: 2026-11-23T02:15:00Z through 2026-11-23T03:40:00Z (system UTC times).
- Sources: API request logs, per-region access traces, and ephemeral inference cache purge logs across the affected host pool.
- Sample methodology: randomized stratified sampling across hosts and request IDs, plus deterministic hash-matching for known FinBlue customer identifiers (customer_id hashes supplied by FinBlue team).

Results summary
- Sample size: 5,400 log lines sampled (see finblue_log_sample.csv).
- Hash reconciliation: all known FinBlue customer identifier hashes either did not appear or only appeared in noise-level artifacts (internal test accounts) and were validated against the customer-provided list. Detailed hash list in hash_manifest.txt.
- No outgoing transfers or anomalous large-batch exports observed.

Attachments included: finblue_log_sample.csv (CSV), hash_manifest.txt (hashes and reconciliation notes), methodology_summary.pdf (sampling protocol and scripts used).

Next steps
- We can provide an independent checksum of the samples or an auditor-facing read-only Drive folder if required.
- If FinBlue needs a signed attestation on company letterhead, we can route that through legal.

Cc'ing Rishi and Marissa to add technical context if you want deeper artifact review.

Regards,
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference
ava@redwood.com
+1 650 555 0210

From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
To: Elena Morales <elena@finblue.com>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 24 Nov 2026 14:28:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Request: quick confirmation on log sampling results

Elena — adding a technical note on the exact verification we ran. Forwarding summary to make the audit trail explicit.

On Tue, Nov 24, 2026 at 10:03 AM Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Elena — thanks for the call.
> 
> Short answer up front: our sampling and hash-reconciliation results show no evidence of FinBlue customer records being accessed or exfiltrated during the incident window. See details below and attachments.

Technical verification steps (abbreviated)
1) Sample selection:
   - Deterministic stratified sample: 5% from each host in the affected pool, plus additional sampling on error-code endpoints.
   - Total sampled lines: 5,400; sample file attached.
2) Hash reconciliation:
   - We received HMAC-SHA256 hashes for customer_id and device_id from FinBlue (salted per your guidance).
   - We ran one-way checks (no reversal) against the sampled logs. All matches were cross-referenced to your test account list.
3) KV cache verification:
   - KV cache eviction windows were examined; no persistent entries containing customer_id hashes were present post-incident.
4) Egress audit:
   - No successful S3 PUTs, external transfers, or large outgoing streams tied to the affected instances in the window.

Conclusion: No sampled evidence of customer-record access or data exfiltration. We consider this a low-likelihood outcome given the coverage and methodology, but if you need higher confidence we can:
- Increase sampling coverage to 20% per host; or
- Provide a signed attestation and the underlying artifacts to your third-party auditor (read-only access).

Attachments referenced: finblue_log_sample.csv, hash_manifest.txt.

—
Rishi Malhotra
Staff SRE, Runtime Safety
rishi@redwood.com

preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Kate Reynolds <kate@curavista.com>, Tom Liu <tom.liu@curavista.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 20 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Contract roll-forward: EU edge node + APAC warm-standby onboarding

Hi Kate / Tom —

Thanks again for the productive renewal discussion last week. Attached is a roll-forward proposal that keeps your existing SLA and adds two items we agreed to explore before signing:

- Dedicated EU edge node (single-tenant) to satisfy data residency and audit requirements
- APAC warm-standby capacity (reserved burst pool + automated failover) for planned expansion next quarter

High-level notes:
- Pricing is shown as two separable line items in the attached pricing workbook so you can accept the EU node now and stagger APAC capacity later.
- We propose a 12-month term roll-forward starting 2027-01-01 with a 30% discount on the APAC warm-standby for the first 6 months as an onboarding credit.
- Technical runbook and ownership: Soojin will be the primary technical lead; Marissa will manage onboarding milestones and success metrics.

Could you confirm the residency controls you require for the EU edge (specific country or region)? Also, Tom — can you share any procurement timing constraints? If this looks good, I can loop in Legal to start a light amendment and a DocuSign flow.

Attachments: SOW_CuraVista_v3.pdf, pricing-dedicated-warmstandby.xlsx

Best,
Ava Chen
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0142


From: Kate Reynolds <kate@curavista.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tom Liu <tom.liu@curavista.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Nov 2026 13:45:12 -0000
Subject: Re: Contract roll-forward: EU edge node + APAC warm-standby onboarding

Hi Ava —

Thanks for the proposal and the clear split for EU vs APAC line items. A few clarifying questions from our side before we move to legal:  

1) Residency: We need EU data to remain in the EU (preferably Germany or Ireland). Is the edge node single-country pinning, or a multi-AZ within the EU? We also need documented evidence for our auditors (ISO/SOC).
2) Failover / RTO: The APAC warm-standby is intended for traffic surges only — what's the tested failover RTO and RPO for cross-region traffic? We need an SLA addendum if the standby becomes primary.
3) Cost: Can we see a blended comparison: current hosted vs. EU node + warm-standby expected monthly spend at 25% utilization? Your pricing sheet had nominal numbers but a blended example would help our CFO sign off.
4) Timeline: We aim to have EU residency in place before our Q2 2027 pilot in Europe. Can you commit to a target onboarding date?

Also, one more ask — we prefer the EU node to be provisioned under a separate subaccount so our data classification tooling can scope it easily. Is that supported?

Regards,
Kate Reynolds
Director of Platform Ops
CuraVista Health
kate@curavista.com

Date: Fri, 20 Nov 2026 15:03:22 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Contract roll-forward: EU edge node + APAC warm-standby onboarding

Team —

Filing Kate's questions above. Can we get a compact deliverable set to send back to the customer by EOD Monday? Specifically:  

- Soojin: Short technical note that covers EU-country pinning options (Germany v Ireland), deployment model (single-tenant node vs logical tenancy), cross-region replication approach, and failover RTO/RPO with test cadence.
- Marissa: Blended cost example (current spend vs expected with EU node + warm-standby at 25% avg util) and proposed onboarding timeline to meet a Q2 2027 pilot. Also confirm subaccount provisioning workflow.
- Ben: Are there any commercial flexes we should pre-approve (e.g., extended onboarding credit, trial APAC burst free for 30 days)?

I'll incorporate the answers into a short amendment draft and confirm whether Legal needs to redline or if we can do a short SOW addendum.

Thanks —
Ava


From: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 23 Nov 2026 10:22:04 -0800
Subject: RE: Contract roll-forward: EU edge node + APAC warm-standby onboarding

Ava / team —

See quick tech note below to send to Kate:  

1) EU residency options
   - Country-pinned single-tenant node (recommended for auditors): provision dedicated node in Germany (Frankfurt) or Ireland (Dublin). Both support HSM-backed key management and dedicated logging.
   - Multi-AZ EU logical tenancy (lower cost): data stays in EU region but may not provide single-customer isolation required by some auditors.

2) Subaccount & data scoping
   - We can provision a customer subaccount under their organization with separate KMS keys and VPC peering. This meets their data classification requirement.

3) Cross-region warm-standby architecture
   - APAC warm-standby is a reserved pool configured with autonomous warming (KV cache priming + scheduled lightweight synchronization).
   - Failover model: automated DNS switch + state rehydration. Typical RTO in tests: 8-12 minutes for routing and cache rehydrate (dependent on cached prefix sizes). RPO: near-zero for persisted data; ephemeral cache may require rehydration.
   - If standby is promoted to primary for >72 hours, automated pricing fallback applies per the SOW (we'll include an SLA addendum for promotion events).

4) Test cadence and runbooks
   - We propose a staged test: dry-run (no traffic) -> controlled promotion (10% traffic) -> failover drill (full traffic). Each stage has a runbook and rollback plan; schedule ~2 weeks for validation in dev/stage, 1 week for production cutover.

5) Timeline (targeting Q2 pilot)
   - EU node provisioning: 6 weeks from signed amendment (procurement + infra + security review).
   - Warm-standby config: parallel 4 week setup after EU node; end-to-end validation 2 more weeks.

I'll attach the short architecture diagram (eu-node-architecture.png) and a one-page runbook we can include in the SOW.

Soojin Lee
Principal Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference
soojin.lee@redwood.ai

Kate / Tom —

Thanks for the quick review and the helpful questions. We updated the SOW and pricing workbook to reflect:  

- EU single-tenant node (Germany default; Ireland option) with separate subaccount and KMS scoping.
- APAC warm-standby as a separable line item; first 30 days at no charge, then billed only when promoted or when average util >15% for the month.
- Failover SLA addendum that documents tested RTO (8-12 mins) and promotion pricing triggers.
- Blended cost sheet attached per Marissa's request (see pricing workbook).

Next steps:  
1) Please confirm preferred EU country (Germany or Ireland).
2) If approved, I'll initiate a short amendment + DocuSign flow (link below). We can sign the amendment independently of the main MSA amendment if you prefer a narrow legal review.

Team —

Filing Kate's questions above. Can we get a compact deliverable set to send back to the customer by EOD Monday? Specifically:

- Ben: Are there any commercial flexes we should pre-approve (e.g., extended onboarding credit, trial APAC burst free for 30 days)?

From: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 23 Nov 2026 10:22:04 -0800
Subject: RE: Contract roll-forward: EU edge node + APAC warm-standby onboarding

Ava / team —

See quick tech note below to send to Kate:  

1) EU residency options
   - Country-pinned single-tenant node (recommended for auditors): provision dedicated node in Germany (Frankfurt) or Ireland (Dublin). Both support HSM-backed key management and dedicated logging.
   - Multi-AZ EU logical tenancy (lower cost): data stays in EU region but may not provide single-customer isolation required by some auditors.

2) Subaccount & data scoping
   - We can provision a customer subaccount under their organization with separate KMS keys and VPC peering. This meets their data classification requirement.

3) Cross-region warm-standby architecture
   - APAC warm-standby is a reserved pool configured with autonomous warming (KV cache priming + scheduled lightweight synchronization).
   - Failover model: automated DNS switch + state rehydration. Typical RTO in tests: 8-12 minutes for routing and cache rehydrate (dependent on cached prefix sizes). RPO: near-zero for persisted data; ephemeral cache may require rehydration.
   - If standby is promoted to primary for >72 hours, automated pricing fallback applies per the SOW (we'll include an SLA addendum for promotion events).

4) Test cadence and runbooks
   - We propose a staged test: dry-run (no traffic) -> controlled promotion (10% traffic) -> failover drill (full traffic). Each stage has a runbook and rollback plan; schedule ~2 weeks for validation in dev/stage, 1 week for production cutover.

5) Timeline (targeting Q2 pilot)
   - EU node provisioning: 6 weeks from signed amendment (procurement + infra + security review).
   - Warm-standby config: parallel 4 week setup after EU node; end-to-end validation 2 more weeks.

I'll attach the short architecture diagram (eu-node-architecture.png) and a one-page runbook we can include in the SOW.

Marissa / Stephanie — quick debrief following the 11/23 screen (we did 45m: 30m tech w/ Jonas + 15m manager):

TL;DR: Strong infra instincts, good systems tradeoff thinking, needs sharper exposition on kernel-level optimization and lacks recent public code but has internal benchmarks. Recommend move to take-home micro-benchmark before on-site.

Detailed notes:
- Technical (Jonas Weber): solid conceptual answers on batching and KV caching; walked us through a previous design where he reduced p99 by ~18% via smarter cache eviction and fused attention kernels. When pushed for low-level detail on memory layout choices, his answers were high-level (good direction, missing concrete memory diagrams).
- Coding: did not share a public GitHub repo; completed a live whiteboard that indicated familiarity with pointer/arena alloc strategies but not daily driver micro-optimization techniques.
- Manager (Ava): asked about cross-team comms and on-call — Ethan gave clear examples of incident ownership and postmortem writing. Good signals for ownership and prioritization.

Risks / unknowns:
- No recent public code to review
- Not deeply experienced in CUDA kernel tuning (has some exposure but not depth)

Recommendation & next steps:
1) Send a 48–72 hour take-home micro-benchmark (we'll measure throughput on a tiny transformer with a provided harness).
2) If take-home looks reasonable, schedule a final loop (on-site/virtual) focused on kernel-level review with one senior infra engineer (Markus Klein) + perf owner.

Ava

title: Parkview: intermittent session resets after private cutover
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ian Thomas <ian.thomas@parkview.com>
Date: Sun, 22 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Parkview: intermittent session resets after private cutover

Team —

Parkview hit us this morning after the private cutover at ~03:30 PDT. They’re seeing intermittent application-level session resets and 502s when traffic egresses through their inspection firewall. Customer trace shows a path that sometimes traverses the inspection appliance on return and sometimes bypasses it, which is causing TCP RSTs / stale conntrack entries.

Symptoms from Parkview:
- App logs: short-lived TCP sessions, abrupt RSTs ~30–90s after open.
- User impact: intermittent request failures to the inference endpoint (about 6–8% of requests during peak).
- They provided traceroutes and a pcap (attached in their reply).

Immediate asks:
1) Can infra/SRE check conntrack counters and SNAT port exhaustion on the private gateway (Parkview side and our egress)?
2) Can runtime check for any recent kernel-level MTU/GSO/GRO changes on the on-prem edge that might cause fragmentation?
3) Karthik/Marissa: Quick audit for policy-based routing or asymmetric route-redistribution introduced in the cutover. If asymmetric return path is hitting a stateful inspection device, that’s our likely root.

Proposed quick mitigations (if we confirm asymmetric path):
- Add route pinning to keep return traffic symmetric for the affected /24s (short-term).
- Apply MTU clamp on the inspection appliance or enforce MSS clamp on our egress.
- Coordinate with Parkview to temporarily disable stateful inspection for the affected path for 30 minutes to validate.

I’m looping in Parkview Ops on the thread. Timeboxed: can we meet these checks within 90 minutes? I’ll take on coordination with the customer.

Thanks,
Ava
Ava Chen
Escalations Lead, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai

---
Attachment: none (requesting captures from customer)


From: Ian Thomas <ian.thomas@parkview.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Date: Sun, 22 Nov 2026 10:03:14 -0800
Subject: Re: Parkview: intermittent session resets after private cutover

Ava —

Thanks for the quick ping. We started collecting traces as soon as the problem surfaced. Uploaded two files to the shared Drive (link below):
- parkview_traceroute_before_after.txt (shows two return paths differing at hop 7)
- parkview_session_capture.pcap (sample of failed session, ~120 KB)

Observations from our side:
- Forward path (client -> Redwood) is stable.
- Return path (Redwood -> client) alternates between the inspection appliance and the MPLS route; when it hits the inspection device we see RSTs and broken sessions.
- We can run a short config change to disable inspection on the affected subnet for testing (no production change without your sign-off).

We’re available for a call now. Details in Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/parkview-cutover-logs

Ian
Ian Thomas
NetOps Lead, Parkview Health
ian.thomas@parkview.com

---
Attachments: parkview_traceroute_before_after.txt, parkview_session_capture.pcap


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>, Ian Thomas <ian.thomas@parkview.com>
Date: Sun, 22 Nov 2026 12:05:26 -0800
Subject: Re: Parkview: intermittent session resets after private cutover

Quick triage notes after reviewing the pcap and traceroute:

Findings:
- The PCAP shows the SYN/SYN-ACK handshake completes, but at ~34s we see a RST coming from the inspection appliance IP (consistent with a stale conntrack or TCP reset triggered by deep-packet inspection).
- The traceroute sample demonstrates return-path asymmetry: two return-hop patterns, one via MPLS core (symmetric) and one via inspection appliance (asymmetric).
- conntrack counters on the private gateway (we requested live stats from Parkview) show high TIME_WAIT and a burst of new entries coincident with the cutover window.

Hypothesis:
- The inspection appliance is performing stateful inspection and occasionally receives return packets it never saw the forward flow for (due to asymmetry). It then injects RSTs or drops packets, leaving the client-server sessions inconsistent.
- Secondary contributing factor: SNAT ephemeral port reallocation between paths could be confusing the appliance. MTU/GRO/GSO issues look unlikely from the capture but we should still verify kernel params.

Recommended immediate steps (test window):
1) Parkview: Temporarily bypass stateful inspection for the affected CIDR for 20 minutes (test).
2) On our side: pin routes for the affected /26 to force symmetric egress/ingress via MPLS core for the next 2 hours.
3) Increase conntrack_eviction thresholds on the private gateway as a mitigation to reduce premature state expiry.
4) Collect conntrack dumps pre/post test (Parkview will provide).

I can run the route pin and conntrack tuning if SRE approves; Dev, can you confirm there are no routing filters that will reject a pinned route? Vanessa, please prepare the playbook and runbook for the test and rollback.

Marissa
Marissa Cole
Network Reliability, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.ai

---
Attachment: parkview_conntrack_stats.txt (requested)


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ian Thomas <ian.thomas@parkview.com>
Date: Sun, 22 Nov 2026 15:40:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Parkview: intermittent session resets after private cutover

Thanks team —

I ran a quick audit of the BGP export/import policies and there’s a conditional route-map that was introduced during the cutover to advertise the new /26 with a lower local-preference under failover conditions. It looks like the MPLS core and the inspection appliance learned different next-hops for the same prefix depending on which egress was active. That fits the asymmetry pattern.

Action plan (executing now):
- Marissa will apply a temporary static next-hop for the affected prefix at our edge to force symmetric return via MPLS.
- Dev will prepare conntrack parameter bump (net.netfilter.nf_conntrack_max + eviction tuning) and hold until we validate.
- Vanessa will coordinate a 30-minute test window with Parkview to disable inspection on the affected CIDR.
- Ava: please coordinate the customer announcement and confirm the test time (propose start at 16:15 PST).

I’ll monitor BGP and conntrack metrics during the window. If the RSTs stop when inspection is bypassed and route pinning is in place, we push for a permanent fix: remove the conditional route-map that causes split-horizon for this prefix or implement symmetric path enforcement in the MPLS fabric.

Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Principal Infra Engineer, Redwood Inference
karthik.iyer@redwood.ai

---
Attachments: none

4) Collect conntrack dumps pre/post test (Parkview will provide).

I can run the route pin and conntrack tuning if SRE approves; Dev, can you confirm there are no routing filters that will reject a pinned route? Vanessa, please prepare the playbook and runbook for the test and rollback.

Marissa
Marissa Cole
Network Reliability, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.ai

---
Attachment: parkview_conntrack_stats.txt (requested)


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ian Thomas <ian.thomas@parkview.com>
Date: Sun, 22 Nov 2026 15:40:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Parkview: intermittent session resets after private cutover

Thanks team —

I ran a quick audit of the BGP export/import policies and there’s a conditional route-map that was introduced during the cutover to advertise the new /26 with a lower local-preference under failover conditions. It looks like the MPLS core and the inspection appliance learned different next-hops for the same prefix depending on which egress was active. That fits the asymmetry pattern.

Action plan (executing now):
- Marissa will apply a temporary static next-hop for the affected prefix at our edge to force symmetric return via MPLS.
- Dev will prepare conntrack parameter bump (net.netfilter.nf_conntrack_max + eviction tuning) and hold until we validate.
- Vanessa will coordinate a 30-minute test window with Parkview to disable inspection on the affected CIDR.
- Ava: please coordinate the customer announcement and confirm the test time (propose start at 16:15 PST).

I’ll monitor BGP and conntrack metrics during the window. If the RSTs stop when inspection is bypassed and route pinning is in place, we push for a permanent fix: remove the conditional route-map that causes split-horizon for this prefix or implement symmetric path enforcement in the MPLS fabric.

Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Principal Infra Engineer, Redwood Inference
karthik.iyer@redwood.ai

title: Draft MAP: data handling playbook & audit checkpoints (timelines + owners)
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Liam O'Connor <liam@healthsync.ai>, HealthSync Legal <legal@healthsync.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Nov 2026 15:20:00 -0800
Subject: Draft MAP: data handling playbook & audit checkpoints (timelines + owners)

Hi Liam / HealthSync legal team —

Thanks again for the call today. Per our discussion I attached a first-pass Mutual Action Plan focused on the data handling milestones you asked for (retention, logging/audit trails, PHI/PII constraints and verification). This note summarizes the open items and proposed owners so we can align on a target close date.

High-level milestones (proposed):
- Retention policy & enforcement matrix — target: 2027-01-15 — owner: HealthSync legal / ops (dependency: Product sign-off)
- Audit logging & retention of logs (access + token traces) — target: 2026-12-10 — owner: Redwood infra (Ben) — dependency: log destination & retention SLA
- PHI/PII processing boundary & redaction flow — target: 2026-12-22 — owner: HealthSync engineering + Redwood integration (Marissa)
- Evidence bundle for procurement (SOC2 artifacts, KMS statement, encryption configs) — target: 2026-12-05 — owner: Redwood (Ava)

Open questions we need from you to finalize dates/owners:
1) Who at HealthSync will be the retention policy approver (legal or compliance contact)?
2) Confirm preferred log storage (your S3 bucket vs Redwood-managed secure object store) — this affects time to deliver.
3) Any specific PHI categories that must be blocked vs redacted at ingestion?

Next steps I propose:
- You review the attached MAP_v1 and add comment in the Drive link by EOD Monday (2026-11-15).
- We schedule a 30-min handoff with your infra and legal for Thurs 11/18 to confirm the log destination and retention SLAs.

Attachments: MAP_DataHandling_Playbook_v1.xlsx (spreadsheet), Retention_matrix.pdf (matrix)

Thanks —
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0142



From: Liam O'Connor <liam@healthsync.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: HealthSync Legal <legal@healthsync.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Nov 2026 09:08:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Draft MAP: data handling playbook & audit checkpoints (timelines + owners)

Thanks Ava — this is a good starting point. A few quick clarifications from our side: 

- Retention approver will be Priya Menon (Head of Compliance) — I'll add her to the doc.
- We prefer to keep logs in our tenant S3 (encrypted, lifecycle rules already exist). Can Redwood deliver a delivery endpoint and schema by 11/30?
- PHI categories to block at ingestion: full SSNs and raw image DICOMs. Everything else can be redacted or tokenized.

I'll review the MAP in Drive and flag comments. Handoff meeting on 11/18 works — can we do 10:00am PT?

-Liam
Director of Engineering | HealthSync
liam@healthsync.ai

title: Operationalizing streaming funcalls + ranking — recap & next steps
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sam Levin <sam.levin@monarchhealth.com>, Priya Rao <priya.rao@monarchhealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 15:05:00 -0800
Subject: Operationalizing streaming funcalls + ranking — recap & next steps

Hi Sam / Priya — thanks again for taking the time today. Short recap from our call and proposed next steps so we’re all aligned.

Key pain you described
- Clinical triage team needs sub-second follow-up suggestions based on multi-turn intake (streaming) but current endpoint stalls on long context.
- Existing pipeline has a reranking stage (BM25 -> model rerank) that’s adding 200–400ms per request and is brittle for new document types (discharge notes).
- Security needs: logs of requests retained 90 days, and model outputs must be redactable/traceable for audits.

Goals we agreed on
- Prototype a streaming + funcall flow that produces partial responses while calling into a structured-output function to populate triage fields.
- Replace the brittle rerank step with an embeddings + light model reranker (embedding similarity candidate recall + small reranker model) to reduce latency and improve stability.
- Deliver a short pilot activity: 2-week sandbox, integration checklist, and a low-risk canary to validate latency & correctness.

Technical constraints you mentioned
- Max end-to-end P95 latency target: 350ms for simple triage flows.
- Maximum 2k token working set in the client (we will suggest KV caching for static patient snippets).
- SOC2 + SCIM provisioning required before sandbox access.

Immediate next steps (owners & timing)
1) Redwood: share a short integration checklist + sample prompt set (attachment) — by Thu 11/12 — owner: Ava.
2) Monarch: provide a representative triage conversation log (anonymized) + a list of must-have structured fields — by Mon 11/16 — owner: Priya.
3) Tech sync (30m) to walk the SDK changes required for streaming and funcalls: Thu 11/19 (propose 10am PT) — owners: Ben + Sam.
4) Security kickoff for SOC2 evidence & SCIM: week of 11/23 — owner: Sergio.

Follow-ups we’ll include in the proposal
- Option A: streaming + funcall path using Redwood streaming SDK + structured output schema. Estimated infra delta: low (we’ll reuse your pool of small vector store nodes).
- Option B: batching + cached KV approach for longer contexts if streaming cannot meet 350ms in initial pilot.

Attachments: integration-proposal.pdf (proposal outline), sample-prompt-set.csv (example prompts + reranker seed queries).

If that reads correctly, I’ll send a formal sandbox invite and share the Drive folder with the proposal.

Thanks — Ava

--
Ava Chen
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwoodinference.com | +1 (415) 555-0192
https://redwood.inference |  


From: Sam Levin <sam.levin@monarchhealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Priya Rao <priya.rao@monarchhealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 17:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Operationalizing streaming funcalls + ranking — recap & next steps

Thanks Ava — that summary is spot on. A couple clarifications from our side: 

- The 350ms P95 is for the 3–5 field triage flow (the longer flows can be 600–700ms while we still feel comfortable).
- The reranker is currently a heuristic layer that drops many relevant docs; replacing with lightweight reranker + embedding candidates sounds promising.
- We can send an anonymized week of triage logs (approx 1k sessions) but need to scrub PHI — Priya will own.

Agree on the proposed next steps. Confirming Thu 11/19 10am PT works for Ben + your team. Please include a short demo of the streaming SDK behavior (partial tokens + structured funcall JSON) in the sync.

Sam
Director, Clinical Systems
Monarch Health

>

title: Customer key escrow exception request for private edge handoff
preview:
From: Sam Torres <sam.torres@medisafe.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Legal Intake <legal@medisafe.com>
Date: Mon, 9 Nov 2026 14:12:00 -0800
Subject: Request: Exception to key escrow for on-premedge deployment

Hi Ava / Legal team,

Quick note following our technical workshop last week — Medisafe is moving forward with a private-edge deployment that will sit in our hospital partner data centers. The deployment model requires that the customer holds the master key (BYOK) and that Redwood has no ability to decrypt production data. Our security team understands Redwood's standard escrow/escrow-operator model, however we need an exception to the keystore escrow clause in the DPA for this specific deployment.

Main asks:
- Can Redwood confirm a formal exception path to avoid key escrow for on-prem / customer-controlled KMS?
- What operational controls would replace escrow to give us assurance (audit logs, operator access windows, breakglass auditability)?
- Any subprocessors that would need access to the deployed environment and their controls?

We'd like a written summary we can share with our compliance board by Friday. I've copied Priya (legal) and Legal Intake for intake.

Thanks,
Sam
Sam Torres
Sr. Security Program Manager, Medisafe Health

Attachment: Medisafe_DPA_Annex_A.pdf (attachment stub)


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Sam Torres <sam.torres@medisafe.com>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 9 Nov 2026 16:01:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Request: Exception to key escrow for on-premedge deployment

Sam —

Thanks for the clear summary. We're supportive of a model where the customer retains the master CMK in their HSM and Redwood operates against a wrapped/delegated key in the private deployment. To address your specific asks at a high level:

1) Exception path:
- We can include a DPA addendum that permits an escrow exception for deployments that meet predefined technical gates (isolated VPC, no cross-tenant networking, customer-managed KMS with attested HSM).
- The addendum would include explicit operator access constraints (time-boxed maintenance windows, jump-host logging, and MFA-brokered sessions).

2) Operational controls proposed to replace escrow:
- Continuous operator action logging shipped to a customer-owned SIEM (Syslog/CloudWatch endpoint) and immutable audit archives retained for 1 year.
- Breakglass process: emergency access only via OPA-reviewed request with two-person approval and egress captured to an independent audit bucket.
- Proof-of-control evidence: weekly tamper-evident snapshots and signed attestations for the first 90 days post-handoff.

3) Subprocessors & access:
- For on-prem deployments we generally limit subprocessors: our telemetry/observability vendor will process anonymized telemetry; our managed-ops partner (if engaged) signs an OTO addendum restricting key access.
- We'll provide a subprocessor list and per-subprocessor control matrix in the SOW appendix.

Next steps I'd propose: Redwood drafts the DPA addendum + SOW appendix and a 1‑page operational controls summary for your compliance board. Dev can sketch the technical gating criteria and Marcus will produce an operator access runbook excerpt.

I'll start the draft today — copying Dev and Marcus to capture operational details. Is Friday acceptable for the compliance summary?

Best,
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachment: SOW-custkey-exception-draft.docx (attachment stub)


From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Sam Torres <sam.torres@medisafe.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 09:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Request: Exception to key escrow for on-premedge deployment

Folks —

- We can include a DPA addendum that permits an escrow exception for deployments that meet predefined technical gates (isolated VPC, no cross-tenant networking, customer-managed KMS with attested HSM).

2) Operational controls proposed to replace escrow:
- Continuous operator action logging shipped to a customer-owned SIEM (Syslog/CloudWatch endpoint) and immutable audit archives retained for 1 year.
- Breakglass process: emergency access only via OPA-reviewed request with two-person approval and egress captured to an independent audit bucket.
- Proof-of-control evidence: weekly tamper-evident snapshots and signed attestations for the first 90 days post-handoff.

3) Subprocessors & access:
- For on-prem deployments we generally limit subprocessors: our telemetry/observability vendor will process anonymized telemetry; our managed-ops partner (if engaged) signs an OTO addendum restricting key access.
- We'll provide a subprocessor list and per-subprocessor control matrix in the SOW appendix.

title: Transient sync policy, temporary egress window and region-pin commitment
preview:
From: Elena Marquez <dpo@acmecorp.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Acme Legal <legal@acmecorp.com>
Date: Fri, 6 Nov 2026 15:12:00 +0000
Subject: Data residency question: transient sync windows & pinning commitment

Hi Ava,

Thanks for taking this — quick context: Acme is finalising a purchase order for Redwood Dedicated and our DPO/legal teams need clarity on a couple of operational behaviors to sign the DPA. Two items in particular:

1) Transient cross-region sync / failover windows: our architecture requires that customer-controlled data remains pinned to region where possible (EU for EU customers, APAC for APAC). We understand there are transient syncs for disaster recovery and cache rebuilds. Can you confirm the maximum duration Redwood needs for any automatic cross-region replication or prefix/KV cache rehydration for production traffic? Please specify any differences between (a) user-facing routing/failover and (b) background analytics/telemetry.

2) Documentation & audit evidence: we need a short sample of the audit logs that show region-of-origin, timestamp, and KMS key id used for transient transfer, plus your proposed DPA clause language that lists the permitted exception and maximum timeframe.

If you can provide a draft clause and an example audit line we can share with our legal team today, that would be great. We prefer the transient window to be limited to 24 hours for customer-sensitive traffic.

Elena
Elena Marquez
Data Protection Officer, Acme Corp
---
Attachments: none in this message


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Elena Marquez <dpo@acmecorp.com>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 7 Nov 2026 09:08:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Data residency question: transient sync windows & pinning commitment

Elena — thanks for the clear ask and sorry for the slightly long reply. Quick TL;DR up front: Redwood can commit to region pinning by default with a controlled, auditable transient sync window for failover and cache rehydration. We propose the following:

- Failover (user-facing routing and KV cache required to restore active sessions): up to 4 hours per incident.
- Analytics/telemetry and background batch rehydration: up to 24 hours per scheduled or incidental transfer.
- All transient cross-region transfers will be logged, encrypted with customer-specific KMS keys (if configured), and retained in audit logs for at least 90 days.

I've attached a draft DPA clause that captures the permitted exception language and a current subprocessor roster. Priyom and Rishi are looped in to confirm operational constraints and the audit log sample.

Please see the attached: redwood-dpa-transient-egress-draft.pdf, subprocessor-roster-2026-10.csv, audit-log-sample-utc.txt

If you prefer 24 hours for all use-cases as you suggested, we can surface that as an option, but we want to be explicit about the different risk envelopes (user routing vs analytics).

Best,
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0132
Redwood Inference
---
Attachments: redwood-dpa-transient-egress-draft.pdf; subprocessor-roster-2026-10.csv; audit-log-sample-utc.txt


From: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Elena Marquez <dpo@acmecorp.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 8 Nov 2026 13:27:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Data residency question: transient sync windows & pinning commitment

Thanks Ava — a couple of operational clarifications from infra:

- The 4 hour window for user-facing failover assumes the customer has enabled regional pinning and that there is compatible capacity in the failover region. For cold restores (e.g., when entire node pools are rebuilt), some background KV rehydration may stretch closer to 12 hours depending on model sequence lengths and cache hit patterns. We can make 4 hours the SLO for active-session recovery and declare a hard upper bound of 24 hours for background restores.

- KMS handling: If the customer provides a customer-managed key (CMK) in the target region, all transient copies will be encrypted with that CMK. If not, Redwood encrypts with our managed keys and we note the key id in the audit entry. We will add the KMS key id field to the audit example.

- Subprocessors: the attached roster includes cloud provider regions used for transient syncs. We will flag any new subprocessor changes during procurement.

Suggested clause tweak (short): 
\"Transient cross-border processing may occur only to support (i) user-facing continuity (SLO: 4 hours) and (ii) background rehydration/analytics (hard upper bound: 24 hours). All transfers will be logged with region-of-origin, destination region, timestamp, and KMS key identifier. Transfers will be encrypted in transit and at rest. Redwood will provide audit evidence on request and will seek customer approval for any extension beyond 24 hours except in a court order or emergency incident where immediate legal requirements apply.\"

Let me know if Acme wants the SLO and the hard upper bound changed to 24 hours for both cases — we can do that but want it explicit in the DPA.

— Priyom
Priyom Das
Product Manager, Redwood Inference
---
Attachments referenced: subprocessor-roster-2026-10.csv (already attached by Ava); audit-log-sample-utc.txt (includes KMS key id sample)


From: Elena Marquez <dpo@acmecorp.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 9 Nov 2026 10:30:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Data residency question: transient sync windows & pinning commitment

Ava/Priyom — thanks, that helps a lot.

We reviewed the draft clause and the audit sample. Two quick follow-ups:

1) Can you replace the phrase \"hard upper bound\" with \"maximum permitted window\" in the clause text? Legal wants language that is actionable and not open to reinterpretation.

2) We need a one-page audit log sample showing a realistic transient transfer event (fields: event_id, customer_id, region_of_origin, destination_region, start_ts, end_ts, bytes_transferred, kms_key_id, initiating_agent). The attached audit-log-sample-utc.txt covers most but please update to include bytes_transferred in the example and re-send.

Subject: Data residency question: transient sync windows & pinning commitment

Hi Ava,

Thanks for taking this — quick context: Acme is finalising a purchase order for Redwood Dedicated and our DPO/legal teams need clarity on a couple of operational behaviors to sign the DPA. Two items in particular:

1) Transient cross-region sync / failover windows: our architecture requires that customer-controlled data remains pinned to region where possible (EU for EU customers, APAC for APAC). We understand there are transient syncs for disaster recovery and cache rebuilds. Can you confirm the maximum duration Redwood needs for any automatic cross-region replication or prefix/KV cache rehydration for production traffic? Subject: Re: Data residency question: transient sync windows & pinning commitment

Elena — thanks for the clear ask and sorry for the slightly long reply. Quick TL;DR up front: Redwood can commit to region pinning by default with a controlled, auditable transient sync window for failover and cache rehydration. We propose the following:

- Failover (user-facing routing and KV cache required to restore active sessions): up to 4 hours per incident.
- Analytics/telemetry and background batch rehydration: up to 24 hours per scheduled or incidental transfer.
- All transient cross-region transfers will be logged, encrypted with customer-specific KMS keys (if configured), and retained in audit logs for at least 90 days.

I've attached a draft DPA clause that captures the permitted exception language and a current subprocessor roster. Priyom and Rishi are looped in to confirm operational constraints and the audit log sample.

Please see the attached: redwood-dpa-transient-egress-draft.pdf, subprocessor-roster-2026-10.csv, audit-log-sample-utc.txt

If you prefer 24 hours for all use-cases as you suggested, we can surface that as an option, but we want to be explicit about the different risk envelopes (user routing vs analytics).

Best,

Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

From: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Elena Marquez <dpo@acmecorp.com>

Date: Sun, 8 Nov 2026 13:27:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Data residency question: transient sync windows & pinning commitment

Thanks Ava — a couple of operational clarifications from infra:

- The 4 hour window for user-facing failover assumes the customer has enabled regional pinning and that there is compatible capacity in the failover region. For cold restores (e.g., when entire node pools are rebuilt), some background KV rehydration may stretch closer to 12 hours depending on model sequence lengths and cache hit patterns. We can make 4 hours the SLO for active-session recovery and declare a hard upper bound of 24 hours for background restores.

- KMS handling: If the customer provides a customer-managed key (CMK) in the target region, all transient copies will be encrypted with that CMK. If not, Redwood encrypts with our managed keys and we note the key id in the audit entry. We will add the KMS key id field to the audit example.

- Subprocessors: the attached roster includes cloud provider regions used for transient syncs. We will flag any new subprocessor changes during procurement.

Suggested clause tweak (short): 
\"Transient cross-border processing may occur only to support (i) user-facing continuity (SLO: 4 hours) and (ii) background rehydration/analytics (hard upper bound: 24 hours). All transfers will be logged with region-of-origin, destination region, timestamp, and KMS key identifier. Transfers will be encrypted in transit and at rest. Redwood will provide audit evidence on request and will seek customer approval for any extension beyond 24 hours except in a court order or emergency incident where immediate legal requirements apply.\"

Let me know if Acme wants the SLO and the hard upper bound changed to 24 hours for both cases — we can do that but want it explicit in the DPA.

— Priyom
Priyom Das
Product Manager, Redwood Inference

title: Capacity decision & rollout checkpoint for CardioSense Dedicated Pool
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.inference>
To: Thomas Li <thomas.li@cardiosense.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.inference>
Date: 2026-11-07T15:02:00-08:00
Subject: Follow-up: dedicated capacity recap & immediate asks

Thomas — thanks for taking the call earlier. Quick recap from our side so we’re aligned and can move to proposal: 

What you stated as the main pain points:
- Frequent latency spikes during peak inference windows (even with smaller batch sizes) impacting SLA to clinicians.
- Need strong tenant isolation for PHI workflows (multi-tenant model serving today but want dedicated pool).
- On-prem-ish networking requirement: VPC peering + BYOK for KMS; audit logs & retention for 12 months.

Goals you asked us to hit:
- P95 latency target: <= 120ms for 512-token requests in steady-state.
- Baseline throughput: ~300 requests/min steady with burst to 1k reqs/min during overnight backfills.
- Go-live target window: early Jan (you mentioned Jan 8–15).

Preliminary sizing notes (attached sizing worksheet):
- Recommended reserved baseline: 8x A100-equivalent GPUs (quantized 4-bit run for most models).
- Burst pool: 2 GPUs for short-term spikes (auto-burst to shared capacity if required).
- Canary plan: Gradual traffic ramp (10% → 50% → 100%) with automatic fallback to hosted model variant.

SLA & security: we can offer 99.9% uptime SLA for the dedicated pool + SOC2 evidence and audit logs. BYOK + VPC private control plane supported; we’ll include Ops acceptance checklist in the next doc.

Next steps we committed to on call:
1) I’ll share an initial commercial + technical sizing (attached).
2) Schedule a technical deep-dive (security + infra) for the week of Nov 14.
3) Draft mutual action plan for signatures and timeline (first cut attached).

Can you confirm: the go-live target window above still works and the procurement contact is procurement@cardiosense.ai? If anything above is off, call it out and I’ll update the sizing doc.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.inference | +1 (415) 555-0193

-- attachments: call_recording_20261107.mp3; initial_sizing_estimate_cardiosense.xlsx; mudp_draft_cardiosense.pdf

title: Monthly KPI packet + QBR prep — exec notes
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Cc: Tom Brooks <tbrooks@cloudport.io>
Date: 2026-11-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Monthly KPI packet + QBR prep — exec notes

Team — attaching the November KPI packet and a short agenda for next week's QBR prep (attached: 20261101-kpi-dashboard.pdf, qbr-prep-agenda.gdoc). High-level callouts from the dashboard (please scan before EOD Monday):

- Latency: P95 increased ~18% week-over-week driven by long-tail spikes on /chat-route (seen during the 10/24 deploy). Rolling average still within SLO but the tail risks affecting latency SLAs for Dedicated customers.
- Cost: Token spend is on a +14% trajectory month-over-month; model mix shift and a few high-throughput accounts are the main contributors. See cost-breakdown-nov.csv for per-customer run-rates.
- Uptime: 99.93% last 30d. Two short infra incidents (2–3 min each) caused minor error bumps; full RCA in the doc.
- Usage: Active routes up 9%, peak qps increased ~12% at 9:30–11:30 PT on weekdays.

Asks for the QBR / OKR check-in:
1) Marcus — can you confirm the proposed mitigation (cache TTL bump + guardrail for the /chat-route) and whether we can ship a hotfix this week?
2) Rishi — please prepare the one-slide cost breakdown for execs (top 3 drivers + 90-day run-rate).
3) Laura — approval to include a short customer-impact blurb for Brightline Health in the executive summary? Elena is copied.
4) Grace — add a short section on Ops readiness and staffing gaps for next quarter.

Please comment inline on the gdoc if you have slide edits. If no major objections, I’ll finalize the QBR deck Tuesday morning and circulate the exec summary.

Thanks —
Ava Chen
Head of Product
Redwood Inference

Attachments: 20261101-kpi-dashboard.pdf (18 pages, charts + highlights), qbr-prep-agenda.gdoc (live), cost-breakdown-nov.csv (per-account run rates)

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Tom Brooks <tbrooks@cloudport.io>
Date: 2026-11-01T13:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Monthly KPI packet + QBR prep — exec notes

Thanks Ava — quick notes inline.

On Mon, Nov 1, 2026 at 9:12 AM Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com> wrote:
> Team — attaching the November KPI packet and a short agenda for next week's QBR prep...

- Latency: Confirmed that the 10/24 deploy introduced a regression in context handling for the /chat-route which created long tail. Ops rolled back the offending kernel within 40m; we still had cache warming issues afterward.
- Mitigation: I support the cache TTL bump for public routes (suggest +25% for route group A) plus a short-term circuit (reject > X tokens from unverified origins). Ops can ship hotfix Wed EOD if we greenlight.
- Cost: I dug into the cost-breakdown CSV — two enterprise accounts (Brightline and one unnamed) are ~46% of incremental spend. We should propose a pricing/usage review with Brightline in the QBR.

Action items I can take this week:
- Draft the technical appendix slide showing before/after P95 for the hotfix (I'll attach a small chart).
- Coordinate with SRE to get the hotfix milestone on Wednesday.

– Marcus
Senior Engineering Manager, Serving Runtime

Attachment: cost-breakdown-nov.csv (referenced above)

To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>

- Top 3 cost drivers: 1) Brightline bursty usage (40% of MoM increase), 2) model mix shift to larger parameter variant for research tests (+6% cost), 3) Testnet jobs mis-tagged as production (we're quarantining these).
- Proposed ask for execs: approve a short usage review with Brightline (billing adjustment and co-designed throttling) and permission to run an autoscaling tweak experiment for two weeks to reclaim ~8–10% cost.
- OKR check: On track for "Reduce inference unit cost by 12%" — currently at -6% vs baseline; if we approve autoscaling test and Marcus executes cache hotfix, expect to hit ~10–11% by EOY.

Slides I’ll prep:
- Exec one-liner (impact + ask)
- 90-day run-rate chart
- Proposed pricing/ops plan for Brightline (owner: Sales + Finance)

Thanks —
Rishi Malhotra
Product Ops

title: Secure hosting discovery recap & next steps
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Dr. Leila Hassan <leila.hassan@medivar.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 2 Nov 2026 15:08:00 -0700
Subject: Secure hosting discovery recap & next steps

Leila — thanks again for taking time today. Below is a concise recap from our call and proposed next steps so your infra and security teams have a clear path forward. If anything below is inaccurate or missing, please flag and I’ll update.

High-level summary (pain & goals)
- Primary pain: must run PHI workloads on customer-controlled infrastructure with strict auditability and no uncontrolled egress.
- Goal: evaluate Redwood Private (VPC/on‑prem) for LLM-powered clinical summarization (pilot ~Q1 2027) with clear cryptographic controls and SOC2-level evidence.

Current stack & constraints noted
- On‑prem Kubernetes cluster (Rancher-managed) hosting FHIR store and ETL pipelines.
- Storage: Ceph/S3-compatible object store; nightly backups to internal tape archive.
- Network: extremely limited outbound — only allowed egress to vendor APIs on allowlist; strict firewall rules; NAT only via approved jump hosts.
- Identity: Okta SSO in place; SCIM provisioning; RBAC enforced.
- Compliance: must maintain auditable key rotation and BYOK/HSM integration for KMS; procurement will require a completed security questionnaire and SOC2 artifacts.

Success criteria for pilot
- Data never leaves customer-controlled network except to allowlisted endpoints agreed in advance.
- Encryption at rest using customer-managed keys (BYOK) with HSM-backed KMS.
- Traceable audit logs for inference requests and model versioning retained for 12 months.

preview:
From: Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@astercloud.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: AsterCloud Billing <billing@astercloud.com>
Date: Thu, 29 Oct 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Sudden 10x jump in embedding usage (Oct 24-28) — please investigate

Hi Ava,

We just received the October invoice and there is a ~10x spike in embedding-related charges for the period Oct 24–28. Our engineering team confirms we didn’t ramp production traffic — only some internal test runs on the sandbox. We estimate ~12M tokens but the invoice shows ~120M. Can you investigate what was counted as billable tokens and share the raw usage slice for that window?

We need an explanation for procurement and to determine if this is a metering/config issue on our side or something in Redwood’s tokenization/aggregation. We’ve attached the billing alert screenshot.

Thanks,
Marta Lopez
Head of Platform Operations, AsterCloud Inc.
marta.lopez@astercloud.com
+1 415-555-0123

[attachment: billing-alert-oct28.png]

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@astercloud.com>
Cc: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 29 Oct 2026 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Sudden 10x jump in embedding usage (Oct 24-28) — please investigate

Hi Marta — thanks for the flag and the screenshot. I’m looping in Soojin (billing) and Karthik (infra/telemetry). We’ll pull the raw usage export and a sample of requests for the window you called out.

Quick heads-up on what we’ll look for: tokenization differences between chunked documents vs. your expected counting, duplicated requests from CI/test harnesses, and any streaming/chunk replays that could have inflated counts.

Can you confirm whether the sandbox keys were exposed to a shared CI runner around Oct 24 (we’ve seen similar patterns when a load-test job got scheduled accidentally)? If helpful, we’ll DOA a provisional credit while we finish the audit.

I attached a small sample request extractor so you can see the fields we’ll compare.

— Ava
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com
