From: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@astercloud.com>
Date: Fri, 30 Oct 2026 08:46:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Sudden 10x jump in embedding usage (Oct 24-28) — initial telemetry export attached

Hi team — I pulled the initial usage export for AsterCloud for Oct 20–31 and attached the slice (usage-export-20261029.csv). Preliminary observations:

- There is a cluster of requests between Oct 24 02:10–04:20 UTC with unusually high embedding token counts.
- The request metadata shows the same API key used across ~8 different client IPs (likely a CI runner cycling through ephemeral addresses).
- Many of the large-count entries have long 'document' payloads that were chunked into ~2–4 chunks each on the server side and each chunk was counted toward the token tally.

Next steps: Karthik, can you pull a sample of the raw request bodies and the server-side chunking logs for that interval (we need to confirm whether client-side chunking vs server-side chunking is responsible)? Marcus, can you sanity-check whether any retry/backoff behavior or streaming reconnects caused repeated charging for the same logical request?

I’ve CC’d Marta for visibility. If this turns out to be an unintended CI ramp, we should propose a one-time invoice adjustment and provide guidance to avoid repeats.

Thanks,
Soojin Lee
Revenue Operations Manager, Redwood Inference

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@astercloud.com>
Date: Sun, 01 Nov 2026 07:21:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Sudden 10x jump in embedding usage (Oct 24-28) — root cause notes

Folks — I grabbed server logs for the Oct 24 02:00–05:00 UTC window and sampled 150 requests (attached sample-requests-clip.jsonl was expanded on our side). Key findings:

- AsterCloud’s sandbox key appears to have been used by a scheduled job that re-run a document ingestion pipeline with long multi-page PDFs. Those documents were chunked server-side into 1k–2k token segments for embedding. Each chunk triggers an embedding call and is counted independently.
- There were intermittent client retries: we see several 409/timeout-induced retries where the client retried after a short backoff, but because the pipeline did not use idempotency tokens for embed operations, retries resulted in duplicate embedding calls.
- Streaming reconnects: for a small percentage (~4%) of requests the websocket/stream connection dropped and the client resumed by re-sending the last chunk without idempotency. That produced duplicate chargeable calls.

Action items we recommend to AsterCloud:
1) Rotate sandbox keys and lock the CI runner to a narrow IP range or use key restrictions (we can provide a how-to).
2) Add a deduplication idempotency token for batching/ingest jobs so retries are tolerated without duplicate billable calls.
3) Use our recommended chunking heuristics (we'll send a short doc) to reduce number of chunks per document.

From Redwood side, I’m proposing a provisional credit of 75% of the spike delta while we finish the full audit (we applied the provisional note to draft credit note id CN-20261101-P). Marcus — can you confirm the credit calc and whether any tokenization edge-case should be adjusted because of server-side chunking behavior?

-Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Senior Infra Engineer, Redwood Inference

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@astercloud.com>
Date: Sun, 01 Nov 2026 15:08:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Sudden embedding usage — credit proposal and next steps

Thanks — I validated the sample and the proposed credit math. Notes:

- We recomputed billable tokens using our conservative model (counting prompt + chunked doc tokens, collapsing immediate retries within 30s as duplicates where logs indicate identical payloads). Using that method, the spike delta is ~98M tokens. A 75% provisional credit equals ~73.5M tokens credited (I converted tokens to your plan's unit price to build CN-20261101-P).
- I confirmed that streaming reconnects accounted for ~3.9% of duplicates — those are included in the credit calculation.
- Attached: provisional-credit-note-20261101.pdf (internal finance template). If AsterCloud agrees, we’ll post the credit to the Nov invoice schedule and issue a final audit summary within 7 business days.

Marta — once you sign off on the provisional credit we’ll:
1) Apply the credit as a negative line on your next invoice (visible in the console).

Hi Ava,

Can you investigate what was counted as billable tokens and share the raw usage slice for that window?

Subject: Re: Sudden 10x jump in embedding usage (Oct 24-28) — please investigate

Hi Marta — thanks for the flag and the screenshot. I’m looping in Soojin (billing) and Karthik (infra/telemetry). We’ll pull the raw usage export and a sample of requests for the window you called out.

Quick heads-up on what we’ll look for: tokenization differences between chunked documents vs. your expected counting, duplicated requests from CI/test harnesses, and any streaming/chunk replays that could have inflated counts.

Can you confirm whether the sandbox keys were exposed to a shared CI runner around Oct 24 (we’ve seen similar patterns when a load-test job got scheduled accidentally)? If helpful, we’ll DOA a provisional credit while we finish the audit.

From: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>

Cc: Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@astercloud.com>
Date: Fri, 30 Oct 2026 08:46:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Sudden 10x jump in embedding usage (Oct 24-28) — initial telemetry export attached

Hi team — I pulled the initial usage export for AsterCloud for Oct 20–31 and attached the slice (usage-export-20261029.csv). Preliminary observations:

- There is a cluster of requests between Oct 24 02:10–04:20 UTC with unusually high embedding token counts.
- The request metadata shows the same API key used across ~8 different client IPs (likely a CI runner cycling through ephemeral addresses).
- Many of the large-count entries have long 'document' payloads that were chunked into ~2–4 chunks each on the server side and each chunk was counted toward the token tally.

Next steps: Karthik, can you pull a sample of the raw request bodies and the server-side chunking logs for that interval (we need to confirm whether client-side chunking vs server-side chunking is responsible)? Marcus, can you sanity-check whether any retry/backoff behavior or streaming reconnects caused repeated charging for the same logical request?

I’ve CC’d Marta for visibility. If this turns out to be an unintended CI ramp, we should propose a one-time invoice adjustment and provide guidance to avoid repeats.

Thanks,
Soojin Lee
Revenue Operations Manager, Redwood Inference

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@astercloud.com>
Date: Sun, 01 Nov 2026 07:21:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Sudden 10x jump in embedding usage (Oct 24-28) — root cause notes

Folks — I grabbed server logs for the Oct 24 02:00–05:00 UTC window and sampled 150 requests (attached sample-requests-clip.jsonl was expanded on our side). Key findings:

- AsterCloud’s sandbox key appears to have been used by a scheduled job that re-run a document ingestion pipeline with long multi-page PDFs. Those documents were chunked server-side into 1k–2k token segments for embedding. Each chunk triggers an embedding call and is counted independently.
- There were intermittent client retries: we see several 409/timeout-induced retries where the client retried after a short backoff, but because the pipeline did not use idempotency tokens for embed operations, retries resulted in duplicate embedding calls.
- Streaming reconnects: for a small percentage (~4%) of requests the websocket/stream connection dropped and the client resumed by re-sending the last chunk without idempotency. That produced duplicate chargeable calls.

Action items we recommend to AsterCloud:
1) Rotate sandbox keys and lock the CI runner to a narrow IP range or use key restrictions (we can provide a how-to).
2) Add a deduplication idempotency token for batching/ingest jobs so retries are tolerated without duplicate billable calls.
3) Use our recommended chunking heuristics (we'll send a short doc) to reduce number of chunks per document.

From Redwood side, I’m proposing a provisional credit of 75% of the spike delta while we finish the full audit (we applied the provisional note to draft credit note id CN-20261101-P). Marcus — can you confirm the credit calc and whether any tokenization edge-case should be adjusted because of server-side chunking behavior?

-Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Senior Infra Engineer, Redwood Inference

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@astercloud.com>
Date: Sun, 01 Nov 2026 15:08:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Sudden embedding usage — credit proposal and next steps

Thanks — I validated the sample and the proposed credit math. Notes:

- We recomputed billable tokens using our conservative model (counting prompt + chunked doc tokens, collapsing immediate retries within 30s as duplicates where logs indicate identical payloads). Using that method, the spike delta is ~98M tokens. A 75% provisional credit equals ~73.5M tokens credited (I converted tokens to your plan's unit price to build CN-20261101-P).
- I confirmed that streaming reconnects accounted for ~3.9% of duplicates — those are included in the credit calculation.
- Attached: provisional-credit-note-20261101.pdf (internal finance template). If AsterCloud agrees, we’ll post the credit to the Nov invoice schedule and issue a final audit summary within 7 business days.

Marta — once you sign off on the provisional credit we’ll:
1) Apply the credit as a negative line on your next invoice (visible in the console).
2) Deliver the audit CSV showing deduped requests and a short tech note with recommended idempotency headers and chunking limits.

If AsterCloud prefers a one-time refund instead of a credit, Finance can do that but it takes an extra 5–7 business days to process.

— Marcus Lin
Principal Product Reliability Engineer, Redwood Inference

Subject: POC activation dependency matrix & onramp run-through

Liam / CardioHealth team — attaching an activation dependency matrix for the POC onramp. This is a lightweight MAP-focused doc that lays out the minimum sequencing we need to hit a production-like test in your staging (API keys, org + tenant setup, IP allowlist, and VPC peering).

Top-level asks: 
- Confirm the IP CIDR ranges we should add to the allowlist (we've proposed 52.22.44.0/24 and 18.197.128.0/25 as a short-lived window for the first two sprints).

- Org setup: we need a tenant name and an owner email (read/write for first 30d).
- Ephemeral API key pattern: propose 72-hour TTL during eval; confirm acceptable.

Proposed milestone cadence (high level):
1) Org + owner assigned (day 0)
2) Short-lived API key + smoke test (day 1)
3) IP allowlist applied + test (day 1–2)
4) VPC peering (optional, day 3–6)
5) Canary load + evaluation queries (day 7–14)

If you want, Jonas can walk through the matrix with your netops team tomorrow — propose a 30-min slot.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Solutions Engineer — Redwood Inference

- VPC peering: CardioHealth typically accepts incoming peering requests (we prefer to initiate to keep our routing strict). I'll have netops create the request if you provide the Redwood VPC ID and proposed routing rules.
- Org owner: use onboarding@cardiohealth.com as the temporary owner and we will switch to the permanent owner when legal finalizes the tenant agreement.
- Ephemeral keys: 72h TTL is fine for the trial. We will need instructions on rotating keys if you expect rolling restarts.

I'll confirm the peering window after sync with netops; tentatively aiming for Mon Oct 18 to Thu Oct 21.

NetOps team / Liam — thanks. Quick technical plan and the minimal artifacts we'll need from CardioHealth to create the peering:
- Required from CardioHealth (NetOps): your VPC ID, the CIDR you will advertise, and a primary contact for route propagation acceptance.
- Required from Redwood: Redwood VPC ID (redwood-prod-eval-1), suggested routing (no default-route export), and a time window for BGP handshake.

Operational notes: 
- We'll provision an ephemeral API key scoped to the eval tenant with 72h TTL. It will be delivered through a signed Doc link (or HubSpot secure file) and rotated automatically after expiry.
- For the allowlist smoke test: provide a simple curl command we can run from our side to hit your staging endpoint once IPs are added. Sample: curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer <api-key>' https://staging.cardiohealth.com/v1/ping
- If CardioHealth prefers to initiate peering, we can share the Terraform snippet and the Redwood peer ASN. Otherwise, we will open the peering request and share the request id for tracking.

Proposed immediate next steps: 
1) Liam / NetOps confirm peering initiation preference and provide VPC ID (today).
2) CardioHealth open the suggested allowlist windows (if you want us to run smoke tests in the window, tell us the ETA).
3) Redwood publishes ephemeral key and sample curl for smoke (once org owner onboarding@cardiohealth.com is confirmed).

Can we do a 30-min call today at 3:00 PM PT to run through how we rotate keys and how we will validate routing? I will share a short runbook during the call.

Jonas Weber
Network & Integrations — Redwood Inference

title: Annual planning kickoff: anchors, buffer policy, and QBR prep
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: 2026-10-11 09:04 PDT
Subject: Annual planning kickoff: anchors, buffer policy, and QBR prep

Team — kicking this off. High level and asks below; aim is to lock targets and a baseline scenario by the end of week 3 (Oct 29). Attaching the draft timeline and the scenario assumptions doc.

Why now
- We need a single set of anchors for FY27 targets before the product roadmap decouples from capacity assumptions.
- QBR deck needs to be draft-ready for exec review the week of Nov 8.

Primary asks (owners in parens)
1) Propose 3-target bands (Aggressive / Base / Conserv.) for ARR, Net Retention, and Hosting unit cost by Oct 22 (Marcus).
2) Define capacity and hiring assumptions that materially change cost-per-token (Laura + Jonas) — call out any procurement lead times.
3) Draft A/B scenario narrative for a 20% demand shock and a 35% latency-sensitive adoption (Grace).
4) Book a 1.5h cross-functional planning workshop (calendar invite will follow) and a separate 30m QBR dry-run for leadership (Ava).

Attachments: [Attachment: 2026_planning_timeline.xlsx (spreadsheet)], [Attachment: scenario_assumptions_v1.md (text)]

Quick timeline
- Oct 22: targets & assumptions due
- Oct 29: consolidated scenario and risk register
- Nov 2: pre-read to execs
- Nov 8: leadership QBR review

Notes and constraints to consider
- We should bake in a conservative buffer for Dedicated capacity lead times (6–8 weeks) and a second-tier fallback model (smaller quantized variant) for latency-critical customers.
- Call out any SLAs that would require SLA credits or changes to pricing.

I know this is busy — if you prefer to split deliverables and own fewer items, flag it in your reply.

Thanks,
Ava
Ava Chen
Head of Product, Redwood Inference
ava@redwood.com
[Attachment: 2026_planning_timeline.xlsx]


From: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace@redwood.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-10-11 10:18 PDT
Subject: Re: Annual planning kickoff: anchors, buffer policy, and QBR prep

Thanks — good framing. A few clarifying Qs and an initial ask list below.

Clarifying Qs
- For \"unit cost\" do you want per-1k-token blended cost at 95th percentile latency or average cost? I'd recommend 95th.
- Are we assuming current quantization profiles or applying the Optimize recommendations?

Immediate deliverables (Marcus)
- I can roughly sketch target bands by Oct 18 if we assume current profiles, or Oct 22 if we incorporate Optimize changes (I prefer Oct 22 to keep it accurate).
- Preliminary numbers: Aggressive = +55% ARR growth target, Base = +35%, Conserv. = +15% (ballpark — will refine).

Blocking items
- Need a clear headcount plan from infra/procurement for dedicated pools — Laura, can you confirm hiring + procurement lead times this week?

I'll start filling the number sheet and flag assumptions inline.

—Marcus
 Marcus Lin
 Head of Revenue Ops
[Sent from my laptop]

To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas@redwood.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-10-11 15:02 PDT
Subject: Re: Annual planning kickoff: anchors, buffer policy, and QBR prep

Ava / Marcus — quick status from capacity/procurement side.

- Hiring: realistic ramp for infra engineers + ops is +8 FTEs spread across Q1. Recruit + offer cadence suggests start-of-hire in mid-Nov; impact on capacity by late-Q1.
- Dedicated GPU lead time: 6–9 weeks for procurement -> install in cloud partner (conservative is 9 weeks if special hardware).
- Cost note: GPU price decline assumptions in scenario_assumptions_v1.md are optimistic; recommend a Base that assumes a modest 10% unit price drop vs. the optimistic 25%.

Attached a short note with procurement milestones and a quick headcount impact table.

[Attachment: procurement_headcount_note.pdf (doc stub)]

Laura Bennett
VP, Platform Operations
laura@redwood.com

We run a similar playbook and have a simple rule of thumb on buffers: keep a 12-week cost and capacity buffer when you sell Dedicated for enterprise deals to avoid SLA exposure. That aligns with your 6–9 week procurement but gives commercial teams breathing room.

Hi Ava,

Thanks for taking this — Novamed's privacy & security team reviewed the materials from your team and we have a couple of follow-ups specific to our EU-only processing requirement (Schrems II / SCCs scope). Can you please confirm the following for our account:

1) Is production inference, backups, and snapshot storage kept physically within the EU region(s) (we'd prefer eu-central-1 / eu-west-1 only)?
2) Does the control plane (auth / metadata / job orchestration) store any cleartext identifiers or payload outside the EU for our account? If yes, what pseudonymization / encryption controls are applied and where are keys held?

4) Deletion proof: what artifacts can Redwood provide to prove data erasure (deletion manifests, snapshot IDs, audit log entries, checksums)?

From: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@novamed.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-10-11T14:30:00Z
Subject: Re: EU-only processing confirmation + deletion evidence request

Elena — thanks for the questions. Below is a concise, account-scoped response for Novamed; I've focused on the controls and concrete artifacts we can provide. Note: these controls are enabled for Novamed's Dedicated EU-only deployment we provisioned during onboarding. If you were discussing a multi-tenant hosted plan, some control-plane metadata would follow a different path — we confirmed Dedicated EU-only is in place for your tenancy.

1) Physical residency
- Compute and persistent volumes: all inference compute, model caches, and persistent snapshots for Novamed are provisioned in eu-central-1 (Frankfurt) and replicated only to eu-west-1 for cross-az redundancy; no replicas are placed outside the EU.
- Backups & snapshots: on a retention cycle configured for your tenancy (default 7 days for hourly snapshots, 90-day cyclic backup overwrite policy) — all stored in EU S3 buckets under our EU data partition.

2) Control plane behavior and keys
- Control plane: for Dedicated EU-only customers we run a private control plane within your VPC in the EU. Metadata that might include request identifiers is pseudonymized at ingestion and encrypted at rest with a customer-scoped CMK (if you provide one) or our EU-resident CMK by default. No cleartext inference payload leaves the EU.
- Key management: option to bring-your-own-key (BYOK) in an EU KMS (customer-managed) or use Redwood-managed EU CMK. Key operations for BYOK remain in EU.

3) Retention windows (defaults, can be tightened per contract)
- Inference inputs (raw request bodies): ephemeral by default, 24 hours unless flagged for debugging — configurable to 0 retention for strict accounts.
- KV / prefix cache: kept in-memory and in-region; cache eviction policies apply.
- Snapshots: hourly snapshots retained 7 days; full backups subject to your backup policy (we set 90-day overwrite by default).
- Audit logs: stored for 365 days in EU (append-only + write-once logs). We can extend retention on request.

4) Deletion proof and artifacts we can provide
- Deletion manifest (CSV) listing object paths, snapshot IDs, timestamps, and SHA256 checksums of the deleted objects.
- Audit log extracts showing erasure events with operator / automation identity and an immutable sequence number.
- Snapshot lifecycle records and storage lifecycle operations with timestamps (S3 DELETE markers, object version IDs).
- Operator affidavit + compact SCCs addendum stating transfer controls in place (see attached redwood_sccs_addendum.pdf).

Attachments we will include in the bundle: redwood_sccs_addendum.pdf, deletion_manifest_template.csv, inregion_network_diagram.png.

5) Operator access
- Human operator access for Dedicated EU-only customers is restricted by role and requires an approved JIT (just-in-time) access request. Access is logged and requires an auditable justification. For Novamed we have restricted operator access to EU-based operations staff per your contract.
- Emergency access: remote troubleshooting runs via ephemeral SSH bastions with session recording; evidence of sessions is included in the audit extracts.

Next steps:
- Jordan will prepare a sample deletion manifest and a small audit extract for the last 30 days.
- Ava will coordinate delivery and the secure upload.
- We can provide a signed operator affidavit and SCCs addendum as separate PDF attachments.

If you need any of the items signed (DocuSign) or require a prescriptive CSV layout for the deletion manifest to match your internal intake, let us know and we'll adapt.

Regards,
Naomi Feldman
Director, Privacy & Compliance, Redwood Inference
naomi.feldman@redwood.ai

Attachments: redwood_sccs_addendum.pdf (pdf, 112KB); deletion_manifest_template.csv (csv, 3KB); inregion_network_diagram.png (png, 98KB)

title: Post-call: StratumAI throughput & latency diagnostic and next steps
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Sanjay Mehta <sanjay@stratumai.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 05 Oct 2026 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Post-call: StratumAI throughput & latency diagnostic and next steps

Hi Sanjay — thanks again for hopping on earlier. Quick written recap and concrete next steps so we’re aligned. I’ve attached the sample payload set you mentioned (stratum_payload_samples.json) and the QPS snapshot CSV.

Summary of what we heard today
- Primary pain: interactive latency spikes during peak ingress (not steady-state). Customers see long tails at the 95th/99th percentiles even when p50 looks OK.
- Secondary pain: throughput collapse when a meaningful fraction of requests are long (>=1024 tokens) and when KV-cache warmup is poor.
- Constraint: data residency for logs and audit trails (SOC2 / enterprise). You confirmed deployment needs to be in us-west2 with VPC peering.

Goals you called out
- Interactive UX: target p95 <= 350 ms for short-to-medium prompts (<=256 tokens).
- Throughput: sustained 40–60 concurrent QPS for typical bursts, with ability to scale to 120 QPS short-term for campaign spikes.
- Quality: retain current model output fidelity; avoid regressions vs your existing Stratum-Lite model.

Initial topology proposal (high-level)
- Dedicated pool (warm path) sized for steady traffic + small hosted fallback pool for overflow.
- Prefix/KV cache for repeated context to cut down transformer work on common prompts. 60s–300s TTL depending on prompt churn.
- Model variants: run primary (Stratum-Lite / equivalent ~13B) and a quantized fallback for cost-sensitive routes.
- Autoscaling: policy by tail-latency and queue length; controlled burst provision (reserve hot slots).

What we need from you to size the POC and benchmark accurately
1) Representative request samples (attached sample set is our placeholder; please confirm or replace).
2) Historical p50/p95/p99 histograms and a short 1-hour sample trace around a peak spike (timestamps + request ids).
3) Expected token-length distribution (percentiles or buckets).
4) Any blacklists/filters for telemetry we should exclude from shared logs for privacy/compliance.

Proposed next steps
- You: send the 1-hour peak trace + CSV of request shape by EOD Wed 10/7.
- Redwood: produce an initial benchmark plan and a recommended Dedicated pool sizing by Fri 10/9.
- Schedule a 60m POC kickoff (we suggested next Tue/Wed); we’ll run three benchmark passes (baseline, quantized, caching-on).

Attachments: stratum_payload_samples.json, stratum-qps-snapshot.csv

Thanks — I’ll follow up once we get your traces and can lock the POC window.

—
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com
Redwood Inference



From: Sanjay Mehta <sanjay@stratumai.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: eng+ops@stratumai.com
Date: Mon, 05 Oct 2026 17:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: StratumAI throughput & latency diagnostic and next steps

Thanks Ava — great write-up. Quick confirmations and I’ve attached the two files you requested (stratum_payload_samples.json and stratum-qps-snapshot.csv).

Confirmations / clarifications:
- Our internal SLA goal for public-facing chat is 99th < 600 ms, but product team prioritizes p95 <= 300 ms for the main interactive route.
- Model: we’re currently on Stratum-Lite (proprietary, ~13B equivalence). Average payload today is ~480 tokens, median ~210; long tail for some agents hits >1500 tokens.
- Peak behavior: we see short-lived spikes when campaigns launch — typical ramp is 2–5 minutes, then plateaus for 10–30 min.
- Data: will share 1-hour peak trace with request ids by EOD Wed (10/7). There are a couple fields redacted for PII but should be sufficient.

Proposed POC timing: next Wed 10/14 or Thu 10/15. We can give a 2-hour window for live traffic replay + 1 hour for debrief. Prefer Wed 10/14 10:00–12:00 PT.

I’ll loop in our infra lead (eng+ops) to coordinate VPC peering details and credentials for the benchmark harness.

—
Sanjay Mehta
VP Product, StratumAI
sanjay@stratumai.com

On Mon, Oct 5, 2026 at 03:12 PM Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Sanjay — thanks again for hopping on earlier. Quick written recap and concrete next steps so we’re aligned. I’ve attached the sample payload set you mentioned (stratum_payload_samples.json) and the QPS snapshot CSV.
>
> [original message trimmed for brevity]



From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Sanjay Mehta <sanjay@stratumai.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 06 Oct 2026 09:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: StratumAI throughput & latency diagnostic and next steps

Thanks — a few technical clarifications so we size the Dedicated pool correctly and craft the benchmark plan. I’ll be running the POC bench with our performance team and want to be precise on the inputs below.

1) Traffic shape and baseline numbers (please confirm):
   - Median token length: 210 (Sanjay said median ~210)
   - Mean token length: 480 (long tail drives this up)
   - Target sustained concurrency for benchmark: 50 QPS, with a 120 QPS short burst (2–5 minutes)

2) Target latency SLOs to optimize for (prioritize in order):
   - p95 <= 350 ms for <=256-token requests
   - p99 <= 600 ms overall
   - cold start target for scaled up nodes: < 2s for first requests (we’ll validate via warmup strategy)

3) Suggested bench passes (we’ll run each 3x):
   A) Baseline: current Stratum-Lite model on default settings (no cache, no quantization)
   B) Quantized path: 4-bit quant (if compatible) + kernel-level optimizations (fused attention kernels)
   C) Cache-enabled: prefix/KV cache with 60s TTL and automatic eviction for >5k unique keys

4) Infrastructure sizing estimate (initial proposal for POC):
   - For 50 QPS @ median 210 tokens: 2 x A10G-equivalent dedicated nodes (sized for low-latency small-batch execution).
   - To support 120 QPS burst without >p95 target slippage: add burst reserve of 4 warm slots (hot processes) and enable managed fallback to hosted pool for overflow.

5) Bench metrics we need you to provide in the trace:
   - Request id, arrival timestamp, completion timestamp, token_count, model_variant, success/error code

- Sanjay: confirm token distribution file + share the 1-hour peak trace (we need request ids in CSV).
- Stratum infra: confirm if the model binary supports 4-bit quantization; if not, tell us best compatible quant profile.
- Redwood: we’ll return a concrete node count + bench script and a pre-populated JMeter/Locust config. Link to our bench harness: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1examplebenchmark

Notes on KV cache and correctness
- Cache gives the biggest win on repeated prefixes (common prompts). For correctness-sensitive intents we’ll implement route-level opt-in so we only use cached responses for idempotent/low-risk flows.

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Sanjay Mehta <sanjay@stratumai.com>

Date: Mon, 05 Oct 2026 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Post-call: StratumAI throughput & latency diagnostic and next steps

Hi Sanjay — thanks again for hopping on earlier. Quick written recap and concrete next steps so we’re aligned. I’ve attached the sample payload set you mentioned (stratum_payload_samples.json) and the QPS snapshot CSV.

Summary of what we heard today
- Primary pain: interactive latency spikes during peak ingress (not steady-state). Customers see long tails at the 95th/99th percentiles even when p50 looks OK.
- Secondary pain: throughput collapse when a meaningful fraction of requests are long (>=1024 tokens) and when KV-cache warmup is poor.
- Constraint: data residency for logs and audit trails (SOC2 / enterprise). You confirmed deployment needs to be in us-west2 with VPC peering.

Goals you called out
- Interactive UX: target p95 <= 350 ms for short-to-medium prompts (<=256 tokens).
- Throughput: sustained 40–60 concurrent QPS for typical bursts, with ability to scale to 120 QPS short-term for campaign spikes.
- Quality: retain current model output fidelity; avoid regressions vs your existing Stratum-Lite model.

Initial topology proposal (high-level)
- Dedicated pool (warm path) sized for steady traffic + small hosted fallback pool for overflow.
- Prefix/KV cache for repeated context to cut down transformer work on common prompts. 60s–300s TTL depending on prompt churn.
- Model variants: run primary (Stratum-Lite / equivalent ~13B) and a quantized fallback for cost-sensitive routes.
- Autoscaling: policy by tail-latency and queue length; controlled burst provision (reserve hot slots).

What we need from you to size the POC and benchmark accurately
1) Representative request samples (attached sample set is our placeholder; please confirm or replace).
2) Historical p50/p95/p99 histograms and a short 1-hour sample trace around a peak spike (timestamps + request ids).
3) Expected token-length distribution (percentiles or buckets).
4) Any blacklists/filters for telemetry we should exclude from shared logs for privacy/compliance.

Proposed next steps
- You: send the 1-hour peak trace + CSV of request shape by EOD Wed 10/7.
- Redwood: produce an initial benchmark plan and a recommended Dedicated pool sizing by Fri 10/9.
- Schedule a 60m POC kickoff (we suggested next Tue/Wed); we’ll run three benchmark passes (baseline, quantized, caching-on).

Attachments: stratum_payload_samples.json, stratum-qps-snapshot.csv

Thanks — I’ll follow up once we get your traces and can lock the POC window.

—
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com
Redwood Inference

title: Observability quickstart for field engineering: cost/latency templates
preview:
From: Tom Hayes <tom.hayes@acmehealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-10-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Observability quickstart for field engineering: cost/latency templates

Hi Ava / Grace,

We're putting together a short enablement pack for our field engineering team so they can triage and escalate cost & latency incidents against Redwood endpoints. Can you help with a lightweight \"quickstart\" that includes:
- Per-route alert templates (examples with suggested thresholds)
- A compact dashboard pack (1-page per-route view) showing cost breakdown, tail latency percentiles, and recent regressions
- A short runbook for on-call and a one-page escalation/approval flow for changes to thresholds

Ideal target: a 1-hour handoff + docs and a set of templates we can reuse across customers. We also attached our intake form (acme-request-form.pdf) with expected traffic profiles.

Two quick questions that will inform templates:
1) Do you have canonical threshold guidance for 95th/99th latency buckets vs baseline?
2) Is there an opinionated cost-spike rule (e.g., % week-over-week) you recommend?

Thanks — appreciate any off-the-shelf material we can adapt.

Tom Hayes
Principal SRE, Acme Health
attachments: acme-request-form.pdf

From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
To: Tom Hayes <tom.hayes@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-10-01T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Observability quickstart for field engineering: cost/latency templates

Hi Tom — thanks for the context and the intake form. This is exactly the kind of lightweight kit we can assemble. Proposed scope for the quickstart (deliverables and owners):

1) Quickstart doc (rdw-observability-quickstart-outline.md) — overview, one-page runbook, handoff checklist (owner: me)
2) Alert templates (sample-alert-templates.json) — per-route templates for latency, error rate, and spend spikes (owner: Kimberly)
3) Dashboard pack — a 1-page per-route view with: 95th/99th latency, cost per 1k tokens, token-pathway breakdown, and recent regressions (owner: Marcus)
4) Approval & rollback flow — short decision tree and required approvers for changing thresholds (owner: Product / Legal as needed)

A couple of asks from our side so we don't overfit the templates:
- Can you confirm the 90/95/99 traffic percentiles and a representative peak qps from your intake?
- Any routes you consider "high-cost" or special (e.g., long-context retrieval, multimodal) so we can add callouts?

I'll attach the outline and a starter JSON for alert templates to iterate. Suggest a 30-min kickoff next week to align on acceptance criteria.

Best,
Ava Chen
Product Manager, Observability — Redwood Inference
attachments: rdw-observability-quickstart-outline.md

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-10-02T08:27:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Observability quickstart for field engineering: cost/latency templates

+1 on Ava's scope. A few practical suggestions from the ops side:

- Latency thresholds: we typically start with baseline-based thresholds (baseline = 7d median). Suggested starters: 95th > baseline * 2 OR absolute > 1200ms --> trigger page; 99th > baseline * 3 OR > 2000ms -> page + start incident runbook.
- Cost spike: 30% WoW increase for a route or 2x typical per-1k-token cost in 1h window -> paging alert. Keep a lower-severity (email) alert at 15% WoW for ops review.
- Canary set: include 5 representative routes (low, mid, high cost; short and long context) so dashboards show sensible distributions.

I'll drop a folder in Drive with example templates: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1ObsTemplatesExample — contains a dashboard JSON and alert snippets we use internally.

Kimberly Park
SRE Lead, Redwood Inference

From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-10-03T14:41:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Observability quickstart for field engineering: cost/latency templates

Thanks — adding a couple of things we should include in acceptance criteria for the field kit:

- Dashboard tile: per-route cost breakdown (token compute vs model size vs I/O) + 95/99 latency percentiles for last 1h/24h/7d. Tile should link to trace/excerpt playback for the top 3 call chains in the time window.
- Sampling guidance: default 1% full-trace sampling, but allow 100% sampling for canary routes for 24h during rollouts.
- Regression detection: automated diff on 95th latency week-over-week with a configurable tolerance (start at 15%).
- Ownership & approvals: any change to a route's paging threshold must include a sign-off from Product (product-counsel@redwood.com) if it reduces alert sensitivity for a customer-facing SLA.

I'll create a short Jira (SUP-1294) to track the playbook work and link the templates.

— Marcus
Principal Observability Engineer, Redwood Inference

title: Urgent: Stratos connector failing on customer digest exports — SLA action plan
preview:
From: Elena Park <elena.park@stratos.io>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 3 Oct 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Stratos connector failing on customer digest exports

Hi Ava,

We're seeing a sudden spike in failures from the Stratos<>Redwood connector that powers daily digest exports for a set of our customers. The first alert came in at ~09:05 PDT and is impacting NordicHealth and two other customers — exports are queuing and timing out with a 502 from your API: POST /v1/ingest/stratos-export.

Error snippet (attached): error_trace.log — stack shows repeated HTTP 502 from redwood-prod behind the gateway. We've tried a restart on our side and reproduced the problem from two different cluster regions.

Can your on-call take a look? This is paginating into customers and will breach our SLO for export completion (we're already receiving customer escalations). If you prefer, we can stand up a quick war room.

Attachments: error_trace.log (5.2 KB)

> Elena — thanks for the heads up and the trace. We're on it.

I've started a quick health sweep of the serving fleet and router. Early notes: a subset of requests hitting redwood-prod are returning upstream 502s tied to a recently deployed kernel change that affects attention batching in long-sequence paths. Hypothesis: connector request shape triggers a path that exercises a newer kernel with lower stability under specific sequence-length + batch combos.

Plan of action:
- Immediately re-route Stratos calls to redwood-compat-v2-ql4 (will be done in config in ~10m).
- Flush KV cache shards for the impacted host groups.
- If re-route + cache flush does not reduce error rate to <1% within 30m, escalate to infra rollback (we have a safe fallback snapshot).

I'll post updates in the war room.

Thanks,
Jonas Weber

Subject: Re: Stratos connector failing on customer digest exports — SLA action plan (resolved)


Hi all,

Update from the war room and remediation timeline:
- 12:42 PDT: We routed Stratos traffic to redwood-compat-v2-ql4 and flushed KV cache shards for the impacted hosts. Error rate dropped from ~6% to 0.4% in the next 12 minutes.
- 12:55 PDT: Deployed a targeted config patch that avoids the new attention batching path for connector shaped requests.
- 13:00 PDT: Verified successful exports for the two impacted customers (NordicHealth, MedScale).

Next steps:
- We'll run a full regression test suite against the staging snapshot that caused the regression and open an infra rollback ticket INFRA-1589 if we see broader impact.
- Postmortem: I'll draft an incident postmortem with timelines and proposed SLA credits by EOD Monday and share the Drive link: https://drive.redwood.ai/Shared/Stratos/STRATO-342-postmortem
- Ops alignment: recommend Stratos enable short-term retry/backoff on export job submissions for 24 hours while we monitor.

Can you confirm from Stratos and from NordicHealth that exports look healthy on your side? If all good, we'll close this thread and follow-up with the postmortem.

Thanks for the quick collaboration — attaching the fallback playbook we referenced: redwood-fallback-playbook.pdf

title: Request: provisional compute subsidy while IRB completes - ethics board note
preview:
From: Prof. Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@unibo.it>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Oak Ridge BioLab <datalab@oakridge.edu>
Date: Thu, 01 Oct 2026 15:12:00 +0200
Subject: Request: provisional compute subsidy while IRB completes - ethics board note

Hello Ava,

I'm Elena Rossi, PI for the Computational Biology lab at the University of Bologna. We're preparing a small pilot (30 participants) using a Redwood-hosted model for de-identified symptom synthesis research. Our internal IRB process has taken longer than expected due to a new data minimization clause; IRB will likely be formally approved in ~6 weeks.

We were wondering whether Redwood can grant a provisional compute subsidy / trial-top up so we can complete onboarding and internal evaluation while we await the final IRB. We're requesting 6,000 inference credits or equivalent for 90 days so our students can finish integration testing and the privacy-preserving pre-processing pipeline.

Attached: IRB_provisional_notice_unibo.pdf (signed provisional note from the ethics office) and project_scope_and_datasets.xlsx (high level - all data will be synthetic or fully anonymized).

Happy to provide any additional documentation (data handling SOPs, K-anonymity summary). Our timeline is tight: kickoff workshop on Oct 26 — if you can support provisioning before then, that would be ideal.

Best,
Prof. Elena Rossi
Department of Computational Biology
University of Bologna

Attachment: IRB_provisional_notice_unibo.pdf (pdf)
Attachment: project_scope_and_datasets.xlsx (xlsx)

I looped in Michael (accounts) and Priya (solutions) to confirm what we can provisionally offer while the formal IRB is pending, and to confirm any compliance/usage guardrails we should add. Michael/Priya — quick ask: can Redwood provision a 6,000-credit pilot valid for 90 days under a provisional grant while we hold the final IRB? If so, what minimal controls should we require (e.g., data access restrictions, confirmed anonymization steps, logging)? Elena, if you have a short SOP or the K-anonymity summary you mentioned, please attach it here.

Thanks,

Ava Chen
Customer Success Manager, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwoodinference.com | +1 415-555-0192
Redwood Inference


From: Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 02 Oct 2026 09:34:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: provisional compute subsidy while IRB completes - ethics board note

Ava / Priya — quick internal notes and recommendation.

- Recommendation: Approve a provisional grant of 6,000 inference credits, valid 90 days from provisioning, tied to the account "University of Bologna - Dept. Comp Bio" (deal EDU-2026-047).
- Conditions: (1) We require the provisional IRB notice (they supplied it) and a short signed attestation that only synthetic/anonymized data will be used for the pilot; (2) Redwood logs must be retained for 90 days and accessible to our security team for spot checks; (3) No PHI/PII as defined in our Acceptable Use policy.
- Next steps: Priya, can you confirm there are no model / dataset restrictions from a safety standpoint? If ok, I can approve credit provisioning and create a HubSpot deal task to mark the credits as "provisional grant".

Thanks — Michael
Michael Grant
Enterprise Accounts, Redwood Inference

> On Thu, Oct 1, 2026 at 17:05 Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
> > Hi Elena — thanks for the clear note and for attaching the provisional IRB acknowledgement. We're excited about the use case and wanting to help you hit your Oct 26 workshop.


From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 02 Oct 2026 10:12:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: provisional compute subsidy while IRB completes - ethics board note

Thanks Michael — quick technical check: the pilot as described (synthetic symptom synthesis) is compatible with our hosted models. No model weights banned. Controls I'd add before provisioning:

- Create a read-only API key scoped to the project that limits storage of raw inputs (prefix hashing + token redaction enabled).
- Enable prefix / KV caching so repeated test sets don't consume unnecessary tokens.
- Set a hard token cap per project run and alerting to avoid runaway usage.

Evelyn — if security signs off on log access and the attestation approach, I'm comfortable provisioning the 6k credits.

Ava — I can draft the simple attestation template and a provisioning checklist (2–3 bullet items) to include in the approval email to the PI.

— Priya
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

title: Post-call: staggered technical deep-dive — three-shift scheduling options
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Marta Silva <marta.silva@medsys.io>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 28 Sep 2026 08:15:00 -0400
Subject: Post-call: staggered technical deep-dive — three-shift scheduling options

Hi Marta — thanks again for a great discovery call this morning. Quick recap and suggested next steps so we can lock in a tech deep-dive: 

Summary from call:
- Use case: secure LLM inference for clinical-note summarization and search across patient records (PHI-in-scope).
- Primary constraints: on-prem/private deployment requirement in EMEA, strict audit logging, SLA window for evening batch processing.
- Success metric: 95th pct latency < 300ms for user-facing queries; embedding throughput for nightly jobs.

Suggested next step: 60–90 minute technical deep-dive with our infra and security engineers to walk architecture, compliance checklist, and a short sandbox demo. To make this work for your global teams we sketched a "three-shift" rotating schedule (US / EU / APAC) so stakeholders can attend at reasonable hours on a recurring cadence. Attached is a simple availability grid and one-page proposal for rotating slots.

Proposed windows (first pass):
- US-friendly slot: Tue 10:00–11:30 ET (good for east coast teams)
- EU-friendly slot: Wed 14:00–15:30 CET (aligns with EMEA technical owners)

preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@neonhealth.com>
Cc: Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwood.com>, accounts@neonhealth.com
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Pilot billing cadence and temporary net terms request

Hi Liam —

Great to see the pilot kicked off yesterday. A quick note from finance that will help us get you fully configured in our billing system and avoid any interruptions as usage ramps next month.

Proposal (to keep the pilot low-friction while protecting both sides):
- Temporary Net45 for the pilot period (60 days from first invoice) instead of standard Net30.
- 30% upfront deposit to be invoiced at provisioning (applied to the first invoice).
- Monthly invoices thereafter, itemized by model and token bands.
- Because NeonHealth is VAT-registered in the EU, we need a VAT certificate before we can zero-rate the invoices; otherwise invoices will include VAT and will need to be remitted accordingly.

Attached: redwood-credit-application.pdf to kick off a light credit check; if you can return the VAT certificate (neonhealth-vat-certificate.pdf) we'll flag the account for VAT handling.

If Net45 is a blocker, we can consider milestone invoicing (50% upfront, remainder at milestone) — happy to discuss. Can you confirm what works for you by EOD Thursday?

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Head of Sales Operations
Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com

2) Net45 only if we get usage credits against SLA breaches and we receive the VAT certificate (we will send that over today).

We can do 30% upfront if it's required; finance will need formal confirmation of the deposit invoice and GL code to process.

I'll have accounts@neonhealth.com send the VAT certificate this afternoon. If you can confirm the invoice reference you will use for the deposit, we can route payment quickly.

title: Order Form commercial: invoicing trigger language, tax withholding contingency, and grace-window for commit shortfall
preview:
From: Tom Briggs <t.briggs@stellarsys.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: accounts-payable <ap-finance@stellarsys.com>, legal@stellarsys.com
Date: Mon, 14 Sep 2026 16:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Draft Order Form — invoicing triggers / tax withholding

Ava — thanks for sending over the draft order form. Quick read-through from procurement/finance here. A few items blocking our internal sign-off:  

1) Invoicing trigger language — Section 2.1 says "invoices issued upon delivery of committed capacity". Can you clarify what constitutes "delivery" for staged commits? We need explicit event triggers so our AP system can auto-issue POs.  

2) Tax withholding / gross-up — Our treasury is in EMEA and they may withhold tax on payments. We typically ask for a clause that requires the vendor to provide tax residency docs and either gross-up or provide evidence of exemption. Can your legal team accept a 30-day evidence window (we can provide certifications within 30 days of first invoice)?  

3) Shortfall/grace window — Stellarsys requests a 60-day grace window for initial quarter commit shortfalls (they prefer not to be invoiced for shortfall until after a cure period). Is Redwood willing to accept a limited forgiveness/cure window for Q1 only?  

We can get legal/redlines over if you want to take this to DocuSign after alignment. Attaching our tax residency template.  

Thanks — Tom
Tom Briggs
Head of Procurement | Stellarsys


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Tom Briggs <t.briggs@stellarsys.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 14 Sep 2026 18:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Draft Order Form — invoicing triggers / tax withholding

Tom — appreciate the quick read and the attachment. High level responses below; attaching a short redline for the Order Form (Stellarsys_OrderForm_v3_redline.pdf) that reflects these asks.  

1) Invoicing trigger: we propose to operationalize "delivery" for staged commits as: (a) the model instance is provisioned and reachable, or (b) when Redwood reports 'Activated' in the activation dashboard, whichever comes first. This gives your AP a deterministic event. Redline adds definition under 2.1.a.  

2) Tax withholding: Redwood can accept a 30-day evidence window to validate Tax Residency certificates. If Stellarsys provides the cert within 30 days of the first invoice, Redwood will issue a credit/adjustment for any withholding applied. We cannot contractually agree to gross-up for third-party withholding at scale, but will commit to cooperation and account-level credit adjustments where evidence is provided. Redline updates Section 6 (Taxes) to reflect this flow.  

3) Grace window for initial quarter: we can accept a one-time 60-day cure window for Q1 shortfalls only, capped at 20% of the quarter target, provided the customer notifies Redwood in writing within 15 days of quarter-end and provides a remediation plan. Added a brief clause in Appendix A.  

Two asks from our side: please confirm the AP billing contact and whether Stellarsys requires Net30 or Net45 payment terms (we typically ship Net30 for self-serve and Net45 for enterprise with PO workflows). Also, please return your legal redline in PDF (tracked) so our counsel can incorporate quickly.

If that all looks OK, I'll loop in Legal (Kimberly) to finalize wording and then send to DocuSign. Proposed times for a 30-min call: Tue 09/15 10:00–11:30 PT or Wed 09/16 13:00–15:00 PT.

Best,

Ava Chen
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwoodinference.com
+1 (415) 555-0142

Attachment: Stellarsys_OrderForm_v3_redline.pdf

Redwood is not obligated to gross-up amounts withheld by a third party, but will cooperate in good faith to mitigate double taxation."

Proposed clause (to be inserted in Section 6.2):  

"Customer shall deliver to Redwood official tax residency documentation reasonably acceptable to Redwood within thirty (30) days of the first invoice. Upon receipt and verification by Redwood, Redwood will issue an adjustment or credit equal to the tax amount withheld on such invoice. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Redwood shall have no obligation to gross-up amounts withheld by third parties unless expressly agreed in a separate written addendum."

On the invoicing trigger definition: to avoid circularity, replace "Activated" with a technical event name we can surface ("ActivationSucceeded") and include the activation dashboard link in the Order Form as an authoritative source. I added a short definitional paragraph in the redline PDF.  

If Stellarsys needs gross-up language for a specific jurisdiction, we can review on a country-by-country basis, but that will likely require finance/legal approval and may result in a one-time onboarding fee. Please flag jurisdictions if relevant.

I'll be available for a short sync Wed 09/16 10:30 PT if the times Ava proposed are partially workable.

Thanks — Kimberly

Hi Ava,

Hope you’re well — our renewal with Redwood is coming up (end of month) and the team wants to expand our Dedicated pool. We currently run an 8-GPU reserved pool and would like to commit to 16 GPUs. However, procurement needs a two-month phasing window: start at 12 GPUs month 1–2, then fully scale to 16 for the remainder of the term.

Key asks:
- Updated monthly commit and pricing for 12→16 phased start (12 for first 2 months, then 16)
- Throughput SLOs (tokens/sec or equivalent) and an availability SLA for Dedicated pools
- Any ramp credits or onboarding assistance you can include
- DocuSign/PO flow and procurement contact to finalize order

- Months 3–12 (full term): 16 GPUs equivalent commit — $YY,YYY / month

- Throughput guidance: based on your reported model profile, we project sustained throughput of ~Z tokens/sec per 16-GPU pool under continuous batching; Connor is validating with a sample trace.

Contract & procurement flow:
- We’ll prepare an amendment to your existing order that reflects the phased commit. Once the amendment is signed via DocuSign we’ll schedule the capacity increase and onboarding.

Please confirm whether the phased start (12→16) and the SLA tier above match your expectations. If yes, I’ll generate the DocuSign package and include the commercial redline.

Marissa Cole

title: Re: Production p99 latency spikes — remediation & concession request
preview:
From: Laura Jenkins <laura.jenkins@finovate.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: procurement@finovate.com
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0400
Subject: Production p99 latency spikes — impact to customers

Ava —

Over the last 72 hours we've seen repeated p99 tail-latency spikes on the Payments normalization route. Two downstream microservices timed out yesterday during our evening peak window and we had to roll traffic to a cached fallback, which degraded authorization accuracy for a short period. Our ops team attached a short CSV with the latency snapshots and a timeline.

This is impacting three of our largest merchants and we've been asked for a remediation plan + commercial offset while you investigate. Our renewals committee is watching this closely — we need a clear proposed concession (credit / free month / remediation commitments) and a timeline for the fix.

Please advise next steps and an ETA for a remediation mitigation we can communicate to our customers.

Attachments: finovate_p99_snapshot_sep14.csv (12KB)

Thanks,
Laura Jenkins
Head of Platform Reliability
Finovate Payments
laura.jenkins@finovate.com


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2026 10:05:00 -0400
Subject: Fwd: Production p99 latency spikes — impact to customers

Forwarding Laura's note — Finovate reports sustained tail-latency during evening peaks (attached). They explicitly asked for a remediation plan + commercial offset.

My read: the immediate ask is two-fold: 1) a commercial concession to restore trust (credit or free month) and 2) operational mitigation to stop recurrence (routing change / dedicated capacity window).

Proposed quick structure for exec review:
- Immediate one-month service credit applied to next invoice (covers customer pain window).
- Pro-rated usage credit for any overage during the incident days (auto-offset).
- Offer a 90-day dedicated capacity reservation for their peak window (we staff and pin nodes, limited to their busiest 4 hours/day) at a 25% discount; trialable and reversible.
- Condition: Finovate signs an amendment with limited waiver language covering historical incidents and accepting standard reversion terms.

I attached a draft concession term sheet (proposed_concession_term.docx). Will need legal to confirm wording and finance to confirm math. Karthik — this is renewal-risky; any commercial input? Marcus — any legal/contract constraints we should be aware of for an ad-hoc credit + capacity promise?

Attachments: proposed_concession_term.docx

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Account Lead — Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2026 13:40:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Fwd: Production p99 latency spikes — impact to customers

+1 on Ava's suggested approach. From a commercial perspective:
- Finovate is in late-stage renewal discussions and this is the biggest immediate risk to close.
- A one-month unconditional credit plus the pro-rated offset is the minimum to stabilize the relationship; we should avoid an open-ended blanket concession.
- The dedicated-capacity trial is a strong retention lever (keeps them on Redwood vs. a competitor offering reserved capacity).

Ask for exec approval on the financial cap: recommend capping the concession at $150k total (credit + trial discount) unless leadership signs off on higher. If we can get Legal and Finance to pre-authorize the amendment, I can present to Finovate's renewal committee by EOD tomorrow.

Naomi — please prepare a customer-facing mitigation note summarizing what we'll do operationally and the expected timeline for eliminating the tail spikes. Marcus — if Legal can draft a short amendment with waiver + limited liability language, Karthik needs that to close the loop.

Karthik
SVP, Sales
Redwood Inference


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2026 16:10:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Fwd: Production p99 latency spikes — impact to customers

I've reviewed the draft concession term sheet. Key legal points / redlines:
1) Credit mechanics: specify that credits apply to future invoices and are non-refundable; include a $150k cap unless signed off by CFO.
2) Trial capacity: call this a "reserved-capacity pilot" for 90 days; include a clause for termination with 7 days' notice and SLA measurement windows.
3) Waiver language: limited release for the incidents described and a carve-out for negligence or gross misconduct — we cannot accept an unlimited indemnity.
4) Signature flow: amendments executed via DocuSign; include an explicit acceptance checkbox for the concession and pilot terms.

I've uploaded a redlined doc to the internal drive and created a DocuSign placeholder link for the amendment. If Sales confirms the $150k cap and Finance greenlights the accounting treatment, Legal is ok to proceed.

Related: please run final numbers on the proposed cap vs. expected churn economics.

Marcus Lin
Senior Counsel, Contracts
Redwood Inference


From: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Laura Jenkins <laura.jenkins@finovate.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, procurement@finovate.com, Carlos Ruiz <c.ruiz@finovate.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2026 18:02:00 -0400
Subject: Final: Concession & remediation plan — next steps

Laura / Finovate team — thanks for raising this and for the data you shared. We've reviewed the incident and coordinated across Sales, Legal, and Engineering. This is the remediation and concession package Redwood will extend: 

1) Commercial concession
- One (1) month service credit applied to Finovate's next invoice (non-refundable).
- Pro-rated usage credit for the incident days (applied as invoice offset).
- Total concession capped at $150,000 unless mutually agreed in writing by Redwood's CFO.

2) Operational remediation
- Redwood will provision a 90-day reserved-capacity pilot for Finovate's 18:00–22:00 ET peak window. The pilot will include dedicated nodes, monitoring runbooks, and priority ops escalation.
- Engineering will run traffic-shaping rules and increased KV cache retention for the pilot window; we will provide a mitigation playbook within 48 hours.

3) Governance & sign-off
- We will deliver a short amendment via DocuSign for signature (link below). The amendment contains the credit mechanics, pilot terms, and a limited release for the incidents cited.
- Once signed, credits will be applied to the next billing cycle and the pilot will be scheduled within 7 business days.

DocuSign amendment (placeholder): https://docusign.com/placeholder/ABC123-AMEND
Internal deal link: https://hubspot.redwoodinference.com/deal/DEAL-13472

Please confirm acceptance or propose edits. Karthik: I'll coordinate the customer presentation once we have the signed amendment. Marcus: thanks for the redlines; we accept the $150k cap and the waiver language as discussed.

Laura — thanks again. Hoping this reestablishes confidence with your ops + renewals teams.

Best,
Naomi Feldman
Head of Customer Success
Redwood Inference

---

> Naomi / Ava / Karthik —
>
> Thanks. We appreciate the speed and the clarity. Finovate accepts the package and will sign the amendment; procurement will push the DocuSign to our legal team today.
>
> I'll confirm once countersigned and share any follow-up timelines.
>
> Laura
> Head of Platform Reliability, Finovate Payments

title: Observability export: Splunk HEC + on‑prem SIEM constraints and tracing hooks
preview:
From: Samantha Liu <samantha.liu@novapay.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Novapay SecOps <secops@novapay.com>
Date: Wed, 9 Sep 2026 14:05:00 -0700
Subject: Observability export constraints for on‑prem deployment

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the walkthrough earlier. We’re starting the internal design for integrating Redwood Private (VPC deployment) with our on‑prem observability stack and ran into a few constraints I wanted to call out and get your recommended approach for: 

- Network: Our environment only allows outbound egress to a short, pre‑approved IP range (see attached novapay-allowed-egress-ips.csv). We cannot open arbitrary external endpoints.
- Auth: Everything must be mTLS with client certs or short‑lived tokens issued by our internal broker. We can avoid long‑lived shared tokens.
- Data residency: Logs/metrics/traces containing PII must be redacted before leaving our VPC; we prefer an in‑customer forwarder that sanitizes and pushes to our Splunk HEC cluster.
- Buffering: We cannot depend on continuous internet connectivity — need an on‑prem buffer/queue local to the VPC so data isn't lost during transient outages.
- Tracing: We want to capture Redwood trace IDs to correlate with our service traces. How do you recommend we surface trace hooks from the Private runtime (span/pseudospan exports)?

Can you outline the minimal architecture you recommend (sidecar vs privileged forwarder vs central relay), sample auth flows, and any limitations (rate limits, max batch sizes, TLS cipher requirements)? We’d like to run a small POC and a canary for one service first.

Attachment: novapay-allowed-egress-ips.csv (text/csv)

Thanks,
Samantha Liu
Director, Platform Security
Novapay



From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Samantha Liu <samantha.liu@novapay.com>
Cc: Novapay SecOps <secops@novapay.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 9 Sep 2026 17:18:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Observability export constraints for on‑prem deployment

Samantha — thanks for the clear constraints and the allowed IP list. Below is a concise proposal tailored to your rules, plus a few follow ups so we can scope the POC.

Proposal (high level)
- Deployment pattern: customer‑managed forwarder (lightweight process/sidecar) + optional central relay inside customer VPC.
  - Sidecar: deployed alongside Redwood inference nodes (recommended for strict per‑host redaction). Responsibilities: log/metric/trace collection, PII redaction rules, local buffering to disk (capped), and push to customer relay/HEC.
  - Central relay: a HA pair (VMs or k8s service) that receives sidecar traffic over mTLS, performs batching and rates, and forwards to your Splunk HEC cluster over the allowed egress IPs. This keeps per‑host ops light and centralizes HEC auth.

- Auth & transport:
  - mTLS between sidecars and central relay using customer‑issued client certificates (rotated via your PKI).
  - Between relay and Splunk HEC: mutual TLS + HEC tokens. If you prefer ephemeral tokens, we can support short‑lived signed tokens (Redwood supports validating token expiry).

- Buffering and durability:
  - Sidecar maintains write‑ahead log (disk ring buffer) with configurable retention and backpressure.
  - Relay persists into a local queue (filesystem or Kafka if you want durability beyond single instance).
  - When connectivity to external Splunk drops, relay will retry with exponential backoff and apply backpressure to sidecars.

- Redaction & schema mapping:
  - We’ll provide a redact hook library (the sidecar can run a small Lua/regex pipeline or call your redact service).
  - We’ll provide a field mapping template to convert Redwood runtime logs/traces to your Splunk HEC sourcetypes. See attached splunk-hec-proxy-sample.conf for examples.

- Tracing hooks:
  - The Private runtime can emit an outbound trace export in two flavors: 1) span samples as JSON over HTTPS, 2) lightweight trace references (traceID, spanID, minimal tags) for high‑QPS paths.
  - For correlation we recommend the lightweight reference mode forwarded to the relay; the relay enriches/joins using a short lived index. 
  - We’ve attached tracehook-sample.yaml with recommended sampling and tag filters. Note: full span export increases bandwidth significantly.

Questions / items we need from you before the POC
1) Confirm the IPs from your CSV that will be used for egress and whether NAT IPs are stable.
2) Confirm whether you want sidecar redaction (per‑host) or central redaction (relay). Sidecar is better for strict PII controls.
3) Rate expectations for logs/metrics/traces (events/sec and average payload size) so we can size relay/buffer.

POC plan (proposed)
- Week 0: Cert/key exchange and firewall allowlist update (we provide CSR templates).
- Week 1: Deploy sidecar on two nodes + small relay in staging. Run synthetic traffic and validate mTLS handshake and egress to your Splunk staging HEC.
- Week 2: Canary production service (single service) for 48 hours. Collect metrics and adjust sampling.

Attachments: splunk-hec-proxy-sample.conf (text/plain), tracehook-sample.yaml (text/yaml)

If this looks good I’ll loop in Diego from infra engineering to help with the sample config and Jonas from runtime to confirm trace export limits.

Best,
Ava Chen
Sr. Solutions Engineer — Private Deployments
Redwood Inference



From: Samantha Liu <samantha.liu@novapay.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Novapay SecOps <secops@novapay.com>
Date: Thu, 10 Sep 2026 08:40:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Observability export constraints for on‑prem deployment

Ava — this is great, thanks. A few clarifications: 

- Egress IPs: The CSV is accurate; NAT IPs are stable for the next quarter but we’ll request longer‑term allocations for production.
- Redaction: We prefer sidecar redaction to avoid raw PII leaving any host.
- Rate: Initial service will be ~450 events/s average, peaks ~2.5k events/s; average event ~1.2 KB. Traces: ~350 trace refs/s, full spans only for sampled errors (~1% of traces).
- Cipher policy: We require TLS1.2+ with AES128/256 GCM or CHACHA20_POLY1305; client certs signed by our internal PKI.

Can we get an ETA on the CSR template and a sample mTLS handshake test we can run? Also prefer the POC early next week — does your team have capacity Monday/Tuesday?

Thanks,
Samantha



From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Samantha Liu <samantha.liu@novapay.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwoodinference.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 12 Sep 2026 10:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Observability export constraints for on‑prem deployment

Samantha — we can support Monday POC start. Quick plan + owners: 

1) Cert exchange and handshake test (owner: Diego) — we’ll send CSR templates and a small Go test binary that attempts an mTLS handshake to your relay IPs. Deliverable: success/fail log + TLS cipher list.
2) Sidecar build + config (owner: Jonas) — sidecar binary with redact hook enabled, default retention 24h, ring buffer 10GB for POC. We’ll supply splunk config and a dry‑run mode that prints batch payloads rather than posting.
3) Relay sizing + smoke tests (owner: Ava) — HA relay (2x small VMs), default batch window 5s, max batch 200 events, retry backoff 30s -> 5m. We’ll benchmark at 3k EPS to verify headroom.
4) Observability and rollbacks (owner: Kimberly) — we’ll provide dashboards (latency, queue depth, drop rate) and a rollback playbook.

Next steps:
- Diego will share CSR template and handshake binary by EOD today.
- Samantha: please share a staging HEC endpoint (FQDN/IP + port) and a test cert we can use for the handshake.
- We’ll open JIRA-OPS-1342 and put the POC checklist in the linked Drive doc.

Attachments referenced in thread: splunk-hec-proxy-sample.conf, tracehook-sample.yaml, novapay-allowed-egress-ips.csv

Kimberly Park
Head of Private Deployments — Customer Engineering
Redwood Inference

Team / Sam —

Quick alignment ahead of next-week activation window. We ran a brief readiness scrub with Product and Docs yesterday; three key asks emerged that I want to land before Friday so AtlasCloud can keep the Sept 20 activation:

1) Ownership for the two enablement flows (admin onboarding + runtime tuning) — need clear owners and training slots.
2) Exception escrow proposal — we sketched a 72-hour delay escrow for non-blocking platform items (docs, minor infra tweaks) with a roll-back trigger if SLA-affecting items remain. Need legal/Security OK and an internal sign-off process.
3) Final enablement deck and a single source-of-truth for rollback/runbook.

I attached a working draft of the enablement deck and the template for the exception escrow. Please read the escrow template and call out any non-starters.

Asks: can each of you confirm (A) who will own the enablement lane, (B) three candidate training slots next week, and (C) any absolute no-go items for the escrow? Sam / Procurement — if there are procurement timelines we should bake into the escrow, flag them.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Launch PM, Redwood Inference
ava@redwood.com

Ava — thanks. Quick notes from enablement angle:

Subject: Re: Aligning on Hosted runtime deep-dive — SDKs, streaming & tool/function patterns

Samir — thanks for the quick reply. I can take the streaming/reconnect portion. A few quick notes so we set expectations:
- Streaming is at-least-once delivery by default for the public hosted path; we have mechanisms for prefix caching and token-level resume keys.
- For client reconnects we can walk through both the client-side SDK approach (resume tokens) and the server-side fallback we use when KV cache misses occur.
- We'll include a short snippet showing function/tool call orchestration (sync vs async) and an example of how callbacks are routed back to a small webhook consumer.

I'll attach an early draft of the runtime diagram (runtime-architecture-draft.vsdx) so your infra team can annotate before the session. Small ask: confirm whether you prefer the meeting in PT or ET timezone for your wider team — we can accommodate both.

Rishi

- Go-live target: 2026-11-15 (soft launch with 10% traffic)

- Compliance/retention: need clear guidance on event logs retention and isolation for PHI-adjacent data

Jane

title: Network handoff and environment validation — Nimbus Health private deployment
preview:
From: Sarah Turner <sarah.turner@nimbushealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood-inference.com>
Cc: Infra Admin <infra-admin@nimbushealth.com>
Date: Fri, 05 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Private deployment — initial network info and questionnaire

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for walking us through the private deployment options yesterday. Per our call, attached is the initial environment questionnaire (filled out partially) and a network inventory export from our infra team. We wanted to surface a few open items before the formal onboarding kickoff: 

- Whether Redwood supports AWS Transit Gateway peering or if we should expect VPC Peering only.
- Preferred approach for egress filtering: proxy in the VPC vs. NAT + firewall rules.
- Requirements for certificate provisioning if we use PrivateLink (do you require customer-managed certs?).
- Any minimum MTU/PKT settings you recommend for the KV cache traffic.

We also included a diagram of our current network zones and the CIDR blocks we plan to reserve. Please let us know if anything looks off — we'd like to get the environment validation checklist so we can run a wiring window next week (Mon/Tue preferred).

Attachments: nimbus_private_onboarding_questionnaire.pdf, infra-network-inventory.csv

Thanks,
Sarah Turner
Head of Cloud Ops
Nimbus Health



From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood-inference.com>
To: Sarah Turner <sarah.turner@nimbushealth.com>
Cc: David Kim <david.kim@redwood-inference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood-inference.com>
Date: Fri, 05 Sep 2026 10:08:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Private deployment — initial network info and questionnaire

Hi Sarah —

Appreciate the questionnaire and the network export. I reviewed the attachment quickly and flagged a few items inline. High level answers to your bullets:

- Transit Gateway: Redwood currently supports both Transit Gateway and VPC peering for hosted-private setups. Transit Gateway is preferred when you have multiple VPCs to route through (it simplifies routing), but we can work with single VPC peering if you need a simpler model. We'll want the TGW attachment ID if you go that route.

- Egress filtering: Our recommended pattern for low-latency inference is to keep egress NAT at the VPC edge and use security groups + NACLs for pod-level restricts. If you need deep outbound filtering (URL-level), place a proxy in the private subnet — Redwood's runtime will work fine behind a proxy but we will need outbound connectivity to our metrics and health endpoints (list below).

- Certs for PrivateLink: We accept Redwood-managed certs for the PrivateLink endpoint, but for strict compliance customers you can present a customer-managed cert via ACM. We will document the CN requirements in the wiring guide.

- MTU: No special MTU required for KV traffic; standard 1500 works in most AWS setups. If you have jumbo frames on-prem, let us know and we can validate.

Next steps: I attached our concise "VPC wiring guide" and a checklist that we use during the environment validation run. Can you confirm a 2-hour window on Monday 09/07 09:00-11:00 PT for a wiring test? David will lead the packet-level validation and Lauren will run the rollout checklist.

Outbound endpoints (for firewall/proxy):
- metrics.redwood-inference.com:443
- health.redwood-inference.com:443
- s3-us-east-1.amazonaws.com (for model artifact pulls)

Attachments: redwood-vpc-wiring-guide.pdf

Best,
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood-inference.com



From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood-inference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood-inference.com>, Sarah Turner <sarah.turner@nimbushealth.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood-inference.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood-inference.com>
Date: Fri, 05 Sep 2026 11:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Private deployment — initial network info and questionnaire

Sarah —

Quick follow-up with specifics so your infra team can prep: 

1) Ports/protocols we will exercise during wired validation (both directions where noted):
   - TCP 443 (TLS) outbound to metrics/health endpoints
   - TCP 22 (SSH) inbound to jump host during the wiring window (we'll use ephemeral keys)
   - TCP 2379 (etcd) internal only — no external exposure
   - UDP 123 (NTP) outbound to your NTP pool or upstream (if you block NTP, let us know; we can fallback to system clock sync)

2) CIDR suggestions: Keep a /24 reserved for Redwood runtime services in each AZ (we will provide final list once you confirm AZs). Avoid overlapping with any VPN prefixes used by your partner networks.

3) Packet capture: we'll request tcpdump during the window for one or two flows (we'll provide exact BPF filters). Please confirm storage location and retention for captures (we recommend short retention — 7 days).

If Monday morning is confirmed I'll send a short runbook and the ephemeral key material an hour before the window.

-David
David Kim
Infrastructure Lead, Private Deployments
Redwood Inference

Subject: Re: Private deployment — initial network info and questionnaire
