Monday 09:00-11:00 PT works for us. Infra Admin will provision a temporary jump host and opens the ports listed below. Please note: our compliance team requires that packet captures are stored in the encrypted bucket with access restricted to the joint tech teams. Retention policy will be 7 days as you recommended.

Attachments: nimbus_private_onboarding_questionnaire.pdf, cicd-auth-flow.png

See you Monday —
Sarah

title: Clarify consumption vs first-license surcharge for private VPC deployment
preview:
From: Jordan Ellis <jordan.ellis@mediq.io>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Lina Morales <procurement@mediq.io>
Date: Tue, 01 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Hi Ava / Marissa,

Thanks again for the demo last week. Procurement at Mediq asked for a short write-up about how your pricing works for an on-prem (VPC-hosted) installation — specifically two items: 1) how the first-license "consumption buffer" surcharge works versus ongoing metered consumption, and 2) whether the platform fee is a one-time onboarding surcharge or a recurring monthly charge. They also asked whether we can cap the platform surcharge for the first year.

Could you send a two-line summary that I can drop into our procurement packet? Also attaching the draft SOW we have — we'd like to finalize commercial terms before their Q4 budget approval.

Best,
Jordan
CISO, Mediq Health

Attachment: Mediq_SOW_v2.pdf (application/pdf, 1.1MB)

From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Ellis <jordan.ellis@mediq.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 01 Sep 2026 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Jordan — quick response below and I can follow up with more detail. Short summary for procurement: 

- First-license consumption buffer is a one-time provisioning surcharge (covers reserved capacity, image packaging, and a 30-day burst buffer). It is invoiced on signing and capped at the agreed amount in the SOW.
- Platform fee is recurring (monthly) and covers control-plane, observability retention, and patch orchestration; we can provide a discounted ramp in months 1–12 for customers committing to a 12+ month support tier.

If you want a single-line option to paste: "Initial provisioning surcharge (one-time) + recurring platform fee (monthly), with a first-year ramp discount available under a 12-month commitment."

Two quick clarifications to help negotiations: the "consumption buffer" is not usage billing — after the first 30 days, consumption is metered separately (per-token / per-embedding or per-node-hour depending on chosen licensing). Also, platform fee discounts are typically structured as % off for months 1–12 tied to a minimum committed node count.

I can join a call with procurement Thursday if helpful.

— Ava
Director, Sales Engineering
Redwood Inference

Attachment: license_matrix_ava_review.xlsx (spreadsheet, 120KB)

From: Lina Morales <procurement@mediq.io>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Jordan Ellis <jordan.ellis@mediq.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ethan Rhodes <legal@mediq.io>
Date: Wed, 02 Sep 2026 08:46:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Thanks Ava — that helps. Procurement asks two follow-ups: (A) can Mediq get a firm cap on total first-year platform spend (provisioning surcharge + 12 months of recurring platform fee) so they can include it in their 2027 capital ask, and (B) how do support tiers map to response times and severity definitions (we need a table to include in the procurement packet). Legal will want any cap reflected as an appendix in the SOW.

Can you provide a capped number range (ballpark okay) and the support-tier bullets by EOD Friday?

Thanks,
Lina
Senior Procurement Manager, Mediq Health

From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Lina Morales <procurement@mediq.io>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 13:15:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Lina — draft numbers below (ballpark ranges assuming 3-node private pool, Standard support and a modest token profile):

- One-time provisioning surcharge (consumption buffer): $45k — $60k (covers packaging, IP escrow prep, initial 30-day burst).
- Recurring platform fee (monthly): $6k — $9k per month.
- First-year capped spend (worst case): $117k — $168k (surcharge + 12 months platform fee).

Support tiers (summary):
- Standard: Business hours (M–F, 9–5) support, 8–24 hour response depending on Sev; patching windows scheduled monthly.
- Enhanced: 24/7 on-call for Sev1, 4-hour response for Sev1, 8-hour for Sev2, quarterly health checks and 8 hours of migration assistance.
- Enterprise: Dedicated TAM, guaranteed 2-hour Sev1 response, weekly ops reviews, unlimited runbook updates, and 40 hours of onboarding engineering.

Notes: We can convert first-license surcharge to a 12-month amortized credit if Mediq signs a 12+ month committed contract and pre-pays 6 months platform fee; we can include that as an appendix to the SOW.

Happy to convert these ranges into fixed-line items for the SOW appendix once you confirm node count and desired support tier.

— Marissa
Head of Commercials, Redwood Inference

From: Ethan Rhodes <legal@mediq.io>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Lina Morales <procurement@mediq.io>
Date: Mon, 06 Sep 2026 16:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Marissa / Ava — Legal reviewed and we like the amortization clause idea. Request two edits for the SOW appendix: (1) explicit definition of "provisioning surcharge" line items (engineering hours, packaging, escrow) with a not-to-exceed figure, and (2) a clear SLA table tying support tiers to response times and escalation contacts.

If you can send a redlined appendix by Wednesday we can fast-track our internal approval. Also confirm whether license transfer on hardware failure is included or priced separately.

Thanks — Ethan
Sr. Counsel, Mediq Health

Can you provide a capped number range (ballpark okay) and the support-tier bullets by EOD Friday?

Thanks,
Lina
Senior Procurement Manager, Mediq Health

From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Lina Morales <procurement@mediq.io>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 13:15:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Lina — draft numbers below (ballpark ranges assuming 3-node private pool, Standard support and a modest token profile):

- One-time provisioning surcharge (consumption buffer): $45k — $60k (covers packaging, IP escrow prep, initial 30-day burst).
- Recurring platform fee (monthly): $6k — $9k per month.
- First-year capped spend (worst case): $117k — $168k (surcharge + 12 months platform fee).

Support tiers (summary):
- Standard: Business hours (M–F, 9–5) support, 8–24 hour response depending on Sev; patching windows scheduled monthly.
- Enhanced: 24/7 on-call for Sev1, 4-hour response for Sev1, 8-hour for Sev2, quarterly health checks and 8 hours of migration assistance.
- Enterprise: Dedicated TAM, guaranteed 2-hour Sev1 response, weekly ops reviews, unlimited runbook updates, and 40 hours of onboarding engineering.

Notes: We can convert first-license surcharge to a 12-month amortized credit if Mediq signs a 12+ month committed contract and pre-pays 6 months platform fee; we can include that as an appendix to the SOW.

Happy to convert these ranges into fixed-line items for the SOW appendix once you confirm node count and desired support tier.

— Marissa
Head of Commercials, Redwood Inference

From: Ethan Rhodes <legal@mediq.io>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Lina Morales <procurement@mediq.io>
Date: Mon, 06 Sep 2026 16:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Marissa / Ava — Legal reviewed and we like the amortization clause idea. Request two edits for the SOW appendix: (1) explicit definition of "provisioning surcharge" line items (engineering hours, packaging, escrow) with a not-to-exceed figure, and (2) a clear SLA table tying support tiers to response times and escalation contacts.

If you can send a redlined appendix by Wednesday we can fast-track our internal approval. Also confirm whether license transfer on hardware failure is included or priced separately.

Thanks — Ethan
Sr. Counsel, Mediq Health

title: Speedgate performance follow-up — A/B plan, metrics we agreed
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Costa <elena@fastapps.ai>; CloudOps <cloudops@prospectcorp.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 05 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Speedgate performance follow-up — A/B plan, metrics we agreed

Thanks for the call this morning — quick recap and concrete next steps so we can get the head-to-head tests scheduled. High level: you wanted an apples-to-apples A/B vs your current HyperscaleCloud inference endpoint (HS-API) focused on tail latency and steady-state tokens/sec.

What we covered and agreed:
- Goal metrics (workload: 512-token generative prompts, batch = synthetic mix I sent): p95 <= 180ms, p99 <= 420ms, sustained tokens/sec target >= 400 on a single 80GB GPU for the representative workload.
- Test types: 1) steady-state throughput sweep (tokens/sec), 2) p95/p99 latency under small-burst traffic, 3) cold-start tail characterization.
- Fairness knobs: KV cache disabled for both endpoints, same prompt set, same client-side batching window.
- Deliverables from your side: sample workload (JSON), auth tokens for HS-API test account, and any rate-limiting IP allowlist.

What Redwood will deliver:
- benchmark-config.yaml (attached) — harness config, sampling intervals, percentiles to collect, and pass/fail rules.
- a small helper script and agent to orchestrate runs (we will provide the gist + Docker container).
- a short summary README that maps raw logs to p95/p99 and tokens/sec.

Proposed schedule:
- You share sample-workload.json + credentials by EOD Monday (9/7).
- We spin up comparators and run initial sweep Tue-Wed (9/8–9/9).
- Short readout and decision checklist on Thu (9/10).

Next actions for Elena/CloudOps:
1) Send the workload file and a HS-API test key (or tell us an IP to allowlist).
2) Confirm you’re OK with the p95/p99 thresholds and that we disable caching on both sides.

If that looks right I’ll loop in Rishi to coordinate dedicated infra for the runs and pin a time for the readout.

Thanks,
Ava
Ava Chen
SaaS Account Lead — Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0198

Attachments: benchmark-config.yaml (attachment stub)


From: Elena Costa <elena@fastapps.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: CloudOps <cloudops@prospectcorp.com>
Date: Sat, 05 Sep 2026 11:04:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Speedgate performance follow-up — A/B plan, metrics we agreed

Thanks, Ava — appreciate the clear plan. Quick notes from our side:

- I attached sample-workload.json (simple mix of 60% short prompts, 40% 400–600 token completions). We can tweak if your harness needs different proportions.
- We’ll create a dedicated HS-API test key and post creds to the secure S3 link below by EOD Monday. Request: can Redwood include the exact CLI calls you’ll run so we can mirror them?
- Important: HS-API enforces a per-client QPS cap; I’ll ask CloudOps to remove the cap for the test window (they’re on copy).

Also, please ensure the harness collects tail metrics with 99.9th-percentile sampling for cold starts — we’ve seen meaningful differences there.

Elena
Elena Costa
Head of Platform, FastApps
elena@fastapps.ai | (212) 555-2234

Attachment: sample-workload.json (attachment stub)


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Costa <elena@fastapps.ai>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 07 Sep 2026 08:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Speedgate performance follow-up — A/B plan, metrics we agreed

Perfect, thanks Elena — got the workload and appreciate you coordinating rate-limits with CloudOps. Quick notes on our harness and what we’ll ask infra to do: 

- Harness: benchmark-config.yaml (attached) defines runs at concurrency = [1,4,8,16], tokens-per-request = 512, warmup = 60s, sample window = 120s. We’ll compute p50/p95/p99 and tokens/sec across the sample window and across full run.
- For fairness we’ll disable server-side prefix caching and KV caching on Redwood and ask HS-API team to do the same.
- Quantization/Model parity: we’ll test your production HS-API model variant and Redwood’s equivalent 16-bit FP path, then run an optional quantized pass if you want to see cost tradeoffs. That’s a second-phase test.
- Cold-start probe: we’ll include a separate run where we kill the underlying worker, wait 2m, then send a 30-request burst to capture startup tail.

As soon as CloudOps confirms the HS-API key & allowlist (or S3 link) we’ll schedule the first sweep. Rishi — can you provision the transient test pool (2 x 80GB GPUs) and the monitoring instance? I’ll share the brief runbook and the helper script link.

Thanks,
Ava

Attachment: benchmark-config.yaml (attachment stub)


From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>; Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Cc: Elena Costa <elena@fastapps.ai>
Date: Mon, 06 Sep 2026 14:05:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Speedgate performance follow-up — A/B plan, metrics we agreed

Adding a quick tech checklist so the runs are apples-to-apples (copying Elena so she sees infra asks):

- Kernel / runtime parity: ensure we run HS-API with a single-stream mode equivalent to Redwood’s continuous-batching disabled path. We’ll run Redwood with the same kernel selection profile (baseline: attention-optimizations ON, KV cache = OFF).
- Batching window: set to 0ms (no client-side coalescing) for the baseline fairness sweep; we will also run a separate batching-sensitivity experiment later.
- Quantization: do phase-1 with native FP16 paths; record memory footprint so we can suggest quant profiles afterward.
- Observability: collect per-request trace ids, start-to-first-token, end-to-last-token timestamps, CPU/GPU utilization and NVLink metrics if available.
- Artifact locations: upload raw logs to the Drive folder (link above). We’ll run the analyzer which emits a CSV with percentiles and token-throughput computed per-second.

Small note on fallbacks: if HS-API applies automatic fallback to a smaller model under load, please flag that in the run logs — it will invalidate direct comparisons.

I’ll stand up the analyzer and config today and post the helper gist.

-Neha
Neha Kapoor
Senior Perf Engineer — Redwood Inference
neha.kapoor@redwood.ai
